SheΟΔβ²§β³Δs not bad to have aroundΟΔβ²§¬Π BUTΟΔβ²§¬Π. She wonΟΔβ²§β³Δt shut the fuck
up or go away for long enough for him to breath.

This beat should be a never ending series of interruptions
of his peace, and yet her helpfulness, making him feel overwhelmed and confused
but grateful for the results. If he didnΟΔβ²§β³Δt find humans so difficult, he would
try to hire her to help with sales. Lol 

¬†

Beat 4 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Yes - Ash has a successful market / BUT it was chaotic and
draining, and now a date and The Business Card?! 

Ash has a successful market / BUT it was chaotic and
draining, and nowΟΔβ²§¬Π The Business Card?!

Ash is wrapping up his sales, cleaning up his table, and taking
all the interest cards from the coffee tin and putting them into his zipper
bag. Ash opens the laptop that looks like it came in a bullet proof case (a
military surplus find) and he put emails out to each one thanking them for
stopping by, linking them to his Instagram (that a kid at church set up for
him), and asking them if they want to place an order to email him the details
and he will provide them with a quote and timeframe and ask for their
commitment before he starts. 

He processes most of the cards, and The Business Card from
the same gentleman as yesterday falls out of the pile and lands on the table
next to the laptop. There is no note, no handwritten explanation, just a new
copy of the card. 

Ash didnΟΔβ²§β³Δt remember seeing this guy today, when did he stop
by? How did he make it to the table without Ash knowing or seeing? Was Destiny
so distracting that he wouldnΟΔβ²§β³Δt have noticed? SheΟΔβ²§β³Δs definitelyΟΔβ²§¬Π distractingΟΔβ²§¬Π in
more ways than oneΟΔβ²§¬Π but he should have noticed an odd looking fellow like he
met yesterday. 

(Ash doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt know, but one of the kids who stopped by to
look at his figurines dropped the card in there for the man)

Ash rips the card up, and tosses it in the trash can nearby.
He doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt have any need for odd people showing up with mystery offers, he
wants peace and quite and to live a normal life despite his disability.

Destiny shows up again, and asks him if she can take him to
dinner, on her dime, for all the help he gave her getting to town, and the
mechanic, and giving her an inside view of the market for her storyΟΔβ²§¬Π Her
story!? Why IS she here? This sounds like trouble I donΟΔβ²§β³Δt needΟΔβ²§¬Π. 

Sequel (Reaction Dilemma Decision)

What is the dilemma and how does it force a decision?

Destiny insists that she needs to repay him for his time,
and he is hungry, he had originally intended to get food before the market, and
he only had time for snacks from vendors between thingsΟΔβ²§¬Π and heΟΔβ²§β³Δs probably
going to the townΟΔβ²§β³Δs only restaurant where she is going to be too, so they may
as well go togetherΟΔβ²§¬Π and sheΟΔβ²§β³Δs not so hard on the eyes, and her way with people
was pretty impressiveΟΔβ²§¬Π against his better judgment, he said yes.

Lady: Great, itΟΔβ²§β³Δs a date. 

Ash: A date?

She winks, and rushes off to speak to another vendor, and grabs
Neds arm taking him away from AshΟΔβ²§β³Δs booth, winking at Ash behind NedΟΔβ²§β³Δs back on
the way.

Ash reluctantly admits she kept Ned away, and so he resigns
that this wonΟΔβ²§β³Δt be the most awful way to spend the rest of the evening. 

¬†

Scene A DATE and some stories

(Goal Conflict Disaster)

Goal: Ash wants to have a dinner without issues, try to enjoy an
interesting humanΟΔβ²§β³Δs conversation, and get home without looking or feeling
crazy.

Beat 1 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Yes they have dinner / But no itΟΔβ²§β³Δs not without issues, in fact,
his hallucinations are really bad tonight.

Ash parks his truck and trailer behind the Moose Lodge Bar
and Grill and walks to the front door where he finds Destiny already in a
friendly conversation with the hostess, swapping numbers like they are lifelong
best friends. 

They are seated by the window, near the fireplace, which
Destiny had the hostess clean and ready for her, and Ash doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt need the menu
because itΟΔβ²§β³Δs the only restaurant in town and he has been here mostly his whole
life, but definitely nowhere else for the last ten years. But he gives her time
to look and ask questions of the server, and look again, while he watches the
sunset over the crest of the hill, behind the tree line. He notices the moon
which has been out for a while settle into its place of prominence in the sky
as the sunset fades to dark blue. 

***hallucination, try to look normal***

She orders, he orders his usual without having to say
anything, because the server has known him for years. 

Dialogue ensuesΟΔβ²§¬Π. 

***hallucination, try to look normal***

She notices something is wrong, assumes it is her. He
assures her that it is not, and he is just uncomfortable in public something. 

***hallucination, try to look normal***

They finish eating, pay, and take a walk by the beach. 

They discover something? 

Or his hallucinations worsen? Or they run into the guy with
the business card? OrΟΔβ²§¬Π. ??? 

Whatever it is, Ash finds it interesting but Destiny (a
reporter) finds it fascinating, and she takes it with her to investigate. They
call it a night, and she returns to her hotel and he returns to his home. She
invites him to stay at the hotel but he declines. 

*Could Briar be doing something from a distance that is
turning off the volume on AshΟΔβ²§β³Δs hallucinations?*

¬†

Beat 2 - Yes/But or No/AndΟΔβ²§¬Π 

Ash takes a lot of meds, stays in doors for days, as he
often does, and Destiny calls a few times. He ignores the calls, but texts that
he is not feeling well and in no condition for a conversation. 

She shows up at his house both excited about what she discovered
about the thing they found, and curious to see if he was just putting her off
or if he was really not feeling well. 

She assumes he is physically ill, doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt realize he is
mentally ill. The alcohol on his breath and unkept beard tell her that he is definitely
not feeling well, whatever that means for him. 

But sheΟΔβ²§β³Δs still so insistent her learn what she learned,
that he reluctantly letΟΔβ²§β³Δs her inside as it is too cold to stand on the porch.

Enter ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ The Mystery ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ They learn about a clinic north of town
with strange reports of people gone missing and showing up dead. 

*Ash will later learn that the
people who run this clinic performed tests on him while he was in the military.
They have former test subjects all over the nation, but found Ash especially
promising and decided to set up shop near him to monitor his condition. The doctor
heΟΔβ²§β³Δs been seeing and getting prescriptions from is a plant by this clinic, and
Dr Briar the man who performed these tests on Ash years ago is the man from the
coffee shop. * 

Ash thinks people showing up at a medical clinic and then
being found dead later is obvious. They were ill, the clinic couldnΟΔβ²§β³Δt cure
them, and they died. WhatΟΔβ²§β³Δs the big deal? WhatΟΔβ²§β³Δs the story? 

Destiny pushes back, theyΟΔβ²§β³Δre not just dead, like in their
homes or at the hospital. TheyΟΔβ²§β³Δre found dead in odd circumstances. A guy known
for being a recluse is found dead in the woods without any real hiking gear; a
guy with a fancy home goes missing for months and then is found dead among a
bunch of homeless people; a lady with a lifelong fear of cats is found being
eaten by them in an alley; and in each case, the victim was seen by others to
be relatively stable before their visit to the clinic with no signs of mental
or physical illness other than a mild depression, which is why they went to the
clinic. 

**How does she know all this? SheΟΔβ²§β³Δs the reporter, what are
her sources? News articles about the individuals but no reporter has tied them
all to the clinic. And she checked into police reports, public records, court documents
from when families sued, and kept following the trail. She searched and found
hundreds of random social media posts about these events from their family
members, may of whom mentioned the clinic. However, while no reporter or public
record mentioned the clinic, the social media posts by family members did. but she
noticed the same clinic mentioned in the stories, and the same doctor (the guyΟΔβ²§β³Δs
name from the business card?!) She pulled LLC information from the secretary of
state website and found the home office was just north of AshΟΔβ²§β³Δs town, but they
had clinics in cities throughout the USA and maybe a few internationally.***

¬†

??????????????

Scene (Goal Conflict Disaster) ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Recon

Question: If he doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt come to meet the man, the man or his
associates could show up when Ash isnΟΔβ²§β³Δt looking or ready. It would be better to
face the issue head on. He decides to check out the guyΟΔβ²§β³Δs hotel room, do some

Goal: He wants to figure out if this man is a threat or not
and go back to his normal life.

Beat 1 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ No/And Ash canΟΔβ²§β³Δt find anything out about the man, ANDΟΔβ²§¬Π?

Ash scopes out the hotel, asks a few town locals about any
odd visitors. 

Beat 2 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ No/And He goes to confront the man, he doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt get to AND the man
is dead.

When he finally goes to the hotel room, he approaches to
find the door kicked up and lock busted. He starts to pull his weapon but
realizes he doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt have a gun. He does; however, have a utility knife he keeps
on his belt, so he pulls this. 

As Ash peaks inside the cracked doorway, he sees the
stranger from earlier dead on the carpet, blood pouring over the rug. Ash
rushes in, sweeps for other intruders, finds none, and checks the manΟΔβ²§β³Δs pulse,
there is none. Ash doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt carry a phone, so he starts to look for the hotel
phone. 

Beat 3 ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ No/And He doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt find out why the man is there AND heΟΔβ²§β³Δs under

title: DarrellβÄôs Writing Plan for 2025
text:
IβÄôve written three versions of my first novel, and ran into the same issues in the next two I tried, all three projects landing in the archive. I can write a compelling moment, but not a scene. I started studying the great writing teachers (Randy Ingermanson, KM Wieland, Brandon Sanderson, Ted Dekker, Bill Myers, and now I will add Hilary Layne to that list).¬† Brandon gave me permission to use what works and ignore what doesnβÄôt, for me. Each of them gave me tools I love.

Using all the tools IβÄôve gathered (see my YouTube Storytelling Playlist and my¬†Writing StoryCraft Database¬†and this new content creator I found¬†Subplots CanβÄôt Hurt You (Rules, Principles, and Structure) - YouTube¬†AND¬†Professor Trailer | Rice University Precollege Online Program).¬†


Hilary Layne at¬†¬†The Second Story - YouTube¬†provides a better way to conceive of progress and a better way to conceive of a βÄ€first draftβÄù as more of a sketch than actually βÄ€writingβÄù the prose and dialogue and stuff.¬†













So here is my writing plan for this year (in between other projects and life requirements).¬†






Write plot mini-arcs like Layne teaches, and do so in VERY rough draft none artistic description ways. This is like when a pencil artist begins with broad shapes that donβÄôt look like much to a non-artist but are actually the frame upon which the art will evolve.


Using KM Weiland, I will be placing these arcs inside Scene/Sequel forms, with the sequel completing each arc.¬†


Using IβÄôm not sure which, or maybe all of them,¬†



Each scene must have a goal, even if that isnβÄôt the main story goal, but a smaller goal (Get coffee, sit in that chair, donβÄôt get involved in a discussion, and donβÄôt look crazy). This goal will be the primary goal throughout this mini-arc.


I will use the Yes/But or No/And¬†to set beats in each scene for the goal, and ultimately end the Scene portion with a final¬†Yes/But¬†or¬†No/And¬†which will set up the Sequel, which will end the mini-arc.¬†


The following arc must have a THEREFORE attached, but I may discover I actually needed more arcs between and create new chain links.





Each session, I will review the previous sessionsβÄô work, edit whatever I need while also priming my brain back into that story world, and then I will set out to complete one more mini-arc. More if there is time and capacity, but one is the goal.


I will have things like the W story structure and the plot points from KM Weiland in the back of my mind, allowing for a few Yes/AND or No/But resolves at the appropriate points of the story, but most will follow the increasing tension pathway. Ultimately, these will probably be inserted after this first draft is done.¬†


After a full first draft is done, including minor edits and plot reviews for adding missing mini-arcs, then I will go back and review the plot points, foreshadowing, and other items from story telling that flush out the story elements.¬†


Only when this framework version is in a state that feels solid and complete, will I then go back to actually βÄ€WriteβÄù the scenes in proper prose, dialogue, and scene descriptions.¬†






This is my template:¬†




Scene (Goal Conflict Disaster)


Goal: He/She wantsΟΔβ²§¬Π Beat 1 - Yes/But or No/AndΟΔβ²§¬Π Beat 2 - Yes/But or No/AndΟΔβ²§¬Π Beat 3 - Yes/But or No/AndΟΔβ²§¬Π Sequel (Reaction Dilemma Decision)


What is the dilemma and how does it force a decision?

 Examples here:¬†Yes/And, Yes/But, No/And, and No/But: Narrative Tools for Dynamic Storytelling ~ Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller

title: Writing Practice 01/02/2025 | Angst the happy dragon
text:
*From a conversation about a book Preston was readingΟΔβ²§¬Π this phrase came up. Good prompt.


How does one get ready for their appointment with God?







ΟΔβ²§≈™How does one get ready for their appointment with a god?ΟΔβ²§¬ù LΟΔβ²§β³ΔAng asked. He turned to his dragon, waiting for a response.


Angst, the tiny pet dragon, sat on his perch near the window, soaking the rare winter sunshine through the window. He nodded thoughtfully for a moment. ΟΔβ²§≈™I see your predicament. ItΟΔβ²§β³Δs not an entirely uncommon experience, but itΟΔβ²§β³Δs not exactly a daily experience either. What does one say, ΟΔβ²§Υ€Howdy there diety, howΟΔβ²§β³Δs the weather?ΟΔβ²§β³Δ That would seem an odd start, indeed.ΟΔβ²§¬ù Angst the dragon smiled to himself, amused at the idea.


LΟΔβ²§β³ΔAng crossed the room to stand next to Angst, intending to look out the window, but he caught his own reflection instead. He began fussing over the grey hairs beginning to shoot up in his otherwise jet black hair. ΟΔβ²§≈™IΟΔβ²§β³Δm not sure what one is to say to a godΟΔβ²§¬Π Or, for that matter, why he wants to see meΟΔβ²§¬Π me of all people.ΟΔβ²§¬ù


ΟΔβ²§≈™You fuss too much,ΟΔβ²§¬ù Angst said, then shook his wings and stretch them before hopping onto LΟΔβ²§β³ΔAngs shoulder. ΟΔβ²§≈™LetΟΔβ²§β³Δs go outside and take a walk, that seems to help you regulate.ΟΔβ²§¬ù


ΟΔβ²§≈™YouΟΔβ²§β³Δre a good friend, Angst.ΟΔβ²§¬ù LΟΔβ²§β³ΔAng replied, and then walked outside to the path leading into the forest.

title: Brainstorming a new fictional worldβÄΠ
text:
Brainstorming a new fictional worldβÄΠ








Posted on¬†March 16, 2024¬†by¬†darrellwolfe







IβÄôm not committed to any of this, I havenβÄôt edited any of it, but IβÄôm just putting it out here to simmer in my brain. IβÄôm brainstorming what it might be like to engage in SciFi/Fantasy in ways that are less rigid. I was so stuck on one story for so long, that I still havenβÄôt finished, I think itβÄôs time to put it away and start a new project.


I want to do some world building for a new world. I loved Lord of the Rings by JRR Tolkein, Chronicles of Narnia by CS Lewis, Wheel of Time series by Robert Jordan, He who fights with monsters, by Shirtaloon, Mistborn series by Brandon Sanderson, Neverwhere by Neil GaimanβÄΠ and others. But I want to be inspired by them not derivative. Help me start thinking through what a fictional world of my own might look like.


IβÄôll ponder some questions in the KM Wieland template and some I got from working it out with ChatGPTβÄΠ This is just a start, session oneβÄΠ TBCβÄΠ




Creating a world thatβÄôs inspired by some of the greatest fantasy series while ensuring it remains unique is a fascinating challenge! LetβÄôs start by exploring key aspects of world-building, inspired by the elements you love in those series, but with a twist to make it distinctly yours.


Setting and Geography: Consider the physical layout of your world. Is it a series of interconnected realms like in the Chronicles of Narnia, a vast, diverse continent like Middle-earth, or does it have a unique structure like the vertically-layered society in Mistborn? Think about creating a unique geographical feature that defines your world. Perhaps a world with floating islands, connected by natural bridges of light or energy, housing different cultures and secrets.


Magic System: Each of these worlds has a unique approach to magic. What if your worldβÄôs magic was tied not just to individuals but to the geography itself? Certain locations could amplify or suppress magic, creating strategic locations that are crucial for the control of power. Your magic system could require a balance between taking and giving back, where every spell has a cost to the environment or the caster.


Cultures and Societies: Inspired by the diversity seen in Wheel of Time and Mistborn, you can create various cultures, each with their unique traditions, politics, and relationships with magic. Maybe one culture has a symbiotic relationship with magical creatures, while another is deeply skeptical of magic. Think about how historical events have shaped these societiesβÄô development and interactions.


Conflict: Drawing inspiration from the epic conflicts in your favorite series, consider what central conflict drives your world. It could be a looming disaster, foretold in prophecy, but with a twistβÄîperhaps itβÄôs something thatβÄôs been misunderstood or misrepresented. Your heroesβÄô quest could involve unraveling the truth behind this prophecy.


Characters: Your characters are the heart of your story. Inspired by the depth and growth of characters in your favorite series, think about creating characters with rich backstories and complex motivations. Maybe your protagonist is someone who can navigate between the magical and non-magical worlds, or someone who has renounced magic but is pulled back into it by circumstances.


Themes and Messages: Each of the series you mentioned explores deep themesβÄîgood vs.¬†evil, the power of choice, sacrifice, and redemption. Consider what overarching themes you want to explore in your world. Maybe focus on themes of balanceβÄîbetween magic and nature, tradition and innovation, or individual desires and communal needs.


History and Lore: A rich history adds depth to your world, as seen in Lord of the Rings and Wheel of Time. Create myths, legends, and historical events that have shaped your worldβÄôs current state. Perhaps your world is at the dawn of a new era, moving away from an age of magic into an age of technology, or vice versa.


Unique Creatures and Races: Instead of elves, dwarves, and orcs, think about creating unique races and creatures that inhabit your world. They could have evolved in unique ways due to the worldβÄôs geography or magic system. For example, a race that evolved in floating islands might have developed wings or other means to navigate the skies.


By combining these elements in new and unexpected ways, you can create a world thatβÄôs both familiar and entirely new. Remember, the key to unique world-building is not just in the elements themselves but in how they interact and influence the story you want to tell.


If you are writing fantasy or science fiction, use these questions to start your world-building. Or, for an incredibly in-depth setting interview, check out Patricia C. WredeβÄôs Fantasy Worldbuilding Questions: http://www.sfwa.org/2009/08/fantasy-worldbuilding-questions/


What does the landscape look like?What kind of plants grow there?WhatβÄôs the climate?What kind of animals are present in the world?


What kind of societies are found in this world?What kind of clothing are in style?What moral and religious values define peopleβÄôs world views?What language do they speak?What form of government is currently in place?


How advanced is technology?What forms of long-distance communication are used?What modes of transport are available?How has technology affected entertainment and the arts?How has technology affected weaponry and modes of warfare?How advanced are the fields of medicine and science?


What are the natural laws of this world?Which natural laws are different from our world?Is there a magical force in your world? How does it work? What are its limitations?


What kind of peoples populate this world?Are there different races?How do customs differ between different races and citizens of different districts?Do the ethnic factions get along?


WhatβÄôs the history of this world?How many years of recorded history are available?What historical epochs have shaped society?


Answer these questions just as you did with your Character Interviews. Let your imagination run wild and record every ideaβÄîno matter how extensive and silly it may initially seem.


These terms refer to different subgenres of speculative fiction, each with its own distinct aesthetics, technology, and social themes:


Steampunk: This genre is characterized by a blend of Victorian-era aesthetics and steam-powered technology. It often features an alternative history setting where steam power remains the dominant technological force, leading to inventive machinery and vehicles, along with a mixture of historical elements and anachronistic technological features. Themes can include adventure, exploration, and the impact of technology on society.


Cyberpunk: Cyberpunk is a subgenre set in near-future societies that are typically characterized by advanced technological and scientific achievements, juxtaposed with a degree of breakdown or radical change in the social order. It often explores themes of artificial intelligence, cybernetics, virtual reality, and large corporations overpowering government authorities. ItβÄôs known for its focus on βÄ€high tech and low life,βÄù showcasing the lives of hackers, outcasts, and rebels within dystopian societies.


Dieselpunk: Dieselpunk draws inspiration from the interwar period between World War I and World War II, emphasizing the diesel technology of the time. It combines the aesthetics of the 1920s to 1950s with retro-futuristic technology and a sense of noir adventure. It often features airships, diesel-powered vehicles, art deco architecture, and sometimes a touch of the supernatural.


Solarpunk: Solarpunk imagines a future in which society has overcome many of the ecological challenges we face today and lives in harmony with the environment. It focuses on sustainability, renewable energy (especially solar power), and a positive interaction between humanity and nature. The aesthetic is often bright and colorful, with an emphasis on architectural innovation, green spaces, and technology that improves quality of life without harming the planet.


Just letting my mind free flow with a map creator, here is a close up of a portion of the planet.


Planet/World Name: The Lands?? NoβÄΠ TBCβÄΠ





These names work as Placeholders, I may want to make them less βÄ€on the noseβÄù in the future, or maybe smooth their edges. Yet, the vocabulary could help to bring the ANE worldview into modern readerβÄôs minds? TBDβÄΠ


Aurongale (DawnβÄôs Breeze) βÄ™ A land half forests and half cleared-forest population centers. Trees and wood form a major component of daily life, the people are akin to the loggers and miners of North Idaho. Neutral/Neutral? They care for the forest so it will always be there for them, but they subdue it in modern ways. ItβÄôs a resource, not integrated. If the HaβÄôEretz incorporate nature into their lives, the Aurongales βÄ€useβÄù it to build but in ways that remove it from their daily living. Think βÄ™ Modern 2x4s framed structures vs Log Cabins with living plants inside. Modernization?


Diedusmai (Death People/People of Death) βÄ™ A land of infected swamps, volcanic ash, ruins of cities with long forgotten names. It is rumored there are monsters there. The Great Wall of The Keep was built to keep these people and monsters out of the civilized society of Aurongale. Chaotic-Neutral? Dieselpunk?


Tohu vβÄôVohu (Wild & Waste / The Wasters) βÄ™ Desert dwellers and nomads (akin to AbrahamβÄôs father in law?). They survive from dug-wells for water, caves, hunting, and harvesting atypical plant life (low growing shrubs, cacti, moss from caves, etcβÄΠ glowing magic moss?) Nomadic Peoples, tribal cultures, we not me.


HaβÄôEretz (People of the Land) βÄ™ They have specialized farming techniques that revolutionized food production and distribution. All the peoples of xxx get produce from HaβÄôEretz except the Diedusmai and the Wasters. These are people who live with nature, including it in their life, dwellings, etc. They do not conquer nature, they include it. Think Solarpunk, in harmony with technology and nature.


Shalomim (Peace Bringers) βÄ™ With the most advanced society (advanced technology, scientific achievements, cybernetics, virtual reality the Shalomim believe their society is unmatched by any other. However, corporations rule over individual rights, and while people are able to use the medical facilities, the decisions of medical professionals are often binary and utilitarian ethic. Meanwhile, there are those who exist within the culture who would use technology to fight back. Cyberpunk


Gan HaβÄôElohim (Garden of the gods) βÄ™ The fabels say that the Great Tree was once the center of activity when the gods roamed the land. The people of this land (name tbd) are the religious establishment. Steampunk The Religious Elite that hold to old fashioned values while pushing advancements only they approve ofβÄΠ They look down on people who donβÄôt accept their steam powered items, but they also look down on those who try to take it into further advanced technologies.


There are other lands not on this map, the world will expand eventually to include other peoplesβÄΠ TBCβÄΠ


The peoples of HaβÄôEretz and Tohu vβÄôVohu both have access to magic which they use seamlessly within their cultures. However, the βÄ€modernβÄù societies look down on these peoples are βÄ€simpleβÄù and have done so for so long that they donβÄôt even know that magic exists, and when they see it, they attempt to explain it away with science. Meanwhile, these two societies use magic, but do so differently. The Wasters use magic with sparing technology, believing that βÄ€scienceβÄù somehow taints the magic. Meanwhile, the HaβÄôEretz blend technology and magic seamlessly into one cohesive lifestyle. It is so cohesive, that visitors donβÄôt know or understand the magical components, and since HaβÄôEretz people are the primary food source for the lands and were historically oppressed by the other nations, they keep their secrets to themselves unless/until someone who genuine interest and trustworthiness, in which case they share openly and freely.


The Deadlands have been quiet for 500 yearsβÄΠ but signs show that the volcanoes are active again, and something seems to be moving in the darkβÄΠ


At one point in the pre-historical past, there was a version of this planet called βÄ€EarthβÄù where humans ruled as the only sentient dominant species. That civilization was destroyed due stripping the planet of resources and polluting the planet for short term profits at the expense of long term health of people and planet. A series of diseases, viruses, resistant bacteria, wars, atmosphere destroying weapons, space pollution, solar flairs, food shortages, supply chain shortages, and a general loss of knowledge about how to live on the land off-grid led to the near destruction of the human species.


A remnant remained but were forced into various silos cut-off from each other. It was over a thousand years before any of the human colonies discovered other colonies due to a complete lack of technological infrastructure, with many believing that this age of βÄ€technologyβÄù was a fairy tale. Even then, many human colonies were not rejoined for many hundreds of thousands of years.


In the interim, the fallen worldβÄôs genetic experiments with animals created sentient life forms just before the age of chaos began. Life forms other than homo-sapiens. Also, the realms of magical creatures came out of deep hiding and began to roam the earth repairing the damage.


In the course of hundreds of thousands of years, societies of various sentient beings developed in isolation from each other, then in groups as they found each other. Just as in the 20th and 21st century, some lands were hyper-technological while others were still agrarian, and yet others were still in hunter-gatherer stages.


Even these societies rose and fell with the tides of time, and the histories of each era became the new worldβÄôs myths and legends, and many of those were forgotten in time.


Due to various natural and unnatuarl causes, the earthβÄôs crust moved, reformed, and reshaped, water levels rose and fell during the global warming which was followed by a new ice age and new melting of that ice age as the planet healed itself.


What was is or will beβÄΠ


It is in the chaos of all these things that we find ourselves in a new world, where things are similar, but not, and different, but notβÄΠ


The Lands are a collection of nations around a major ocean on the far side of the planet from any other land mases. Those remain a mystery to me right now, but we may explore those in the future.


For now, The Lands each developed with mixed species sentient beings, but each in various different ways. Humans are the minority here but do make up approximately 24% of the planetary population. They are not isolated from the other species, no society is species specific. They are, however, ideologically and culturally specific.


Each of The Lands have their unique culture and technology and relationship to magic.


Speaking of magic, here are some basics for nowβÄΠ


Magic is essentially like electricity, it can be harnessed to power/empower people and things. Individuals can harness magic in their bodies, but technology can also be empowered by magic.


Some societies reject magic entirely, even rejecting the idea that it is βÄ€realβÄù, preferring a scientific explanation for all things. βÄ€It isnβÄôt magic,βÄù Harvester said, puffing his pipe. βÄ€It is simply not yet explained by our understanding. We know that in the old ages they had more understandings than we have here, so we simply have yet to understand the mechanisms.βÄù


Meanwhile, other societies use magic to varying degrees, or hire magic practitioners from the lands that understand it.


Still other sub-groups make magic the centerpiece of their society, education, study, and livelihoods.


The same can be said of the various levels of technology, ranging from groups that could be called Solarpunk inspired, Dieselpunk, Steampunk, and even Cyberpunk. These blends of technology in each society operate seamlessly on the local-global scene. While it is rare for someone from one society to join another, it is not unheard of, and it is acceptable to various degrees in each society.


Each society also has its detractors, dissidents, rebels, criminals, hackers, freedom fighters, etc. Just as Ireland was fighting for independence and the USA was fighting for independence, but these were not always exactly simultaneously, each society has had its developments asynchronous from each other while still influencing each other randomly throughout their histories.


In the case of The Lands, they were brought together when they fought the monsters (a combination of genetic experiments and evolution gone wrong), a people still operate in that region, the Diedusmai. They were walled off from the people of The Lands who had joined each other as one force to push them back. That created a sometimes tense unity, where each society was allowed to develop as they saw fight without interference from the others, while continuing trade relations.


The Diedusmai havenβÄôt been seen or heard in 500 years, but there is evidence that those lands are awakening againβÄΠ


Meanwhile, we enter out story by learning about the various sentient beings through meeting our cast (protags, antags, and the larger population of supporting roles).


βÄΠ. But who will they be??


Humans (who have evolved in interesting ways, so not quite human in some cases?)Breath under water, gillsExtra eyelidsVery small and very largeEye sight (better and worse, depending on background of that race)All the current races mixed in interesting ways in isolation?Could they have developed wings?


Animal Evolutions:Dolphins & OrcasWolfCat-Like creature?Primate that isnβÄôt humanRats and Bunnies (because theyβÄôre so common in current science theyβÄôre piratically a trope of experimentation).PigsSnakes/Lizards?


Magic Creatures were real afterall?Vampires?Elves?Dwarves?Gnomes?Elohim & ShedimAnukimEntsDo wizards come from entities being taught to harness magic by the Elohim? Ugarit mythologies?

title: Grounded Cafe (take four) - #WhiteNoise #Distortion
text:
Ice raced down AshβÄôs spine, an old familiar call to attention. He turned his head toward movement in his peripheral, was that two hostiles across the street? Through the undusted window of the Grounded Cafe, Ash caught sight of two shadowy figures creeping across the cracked asphalt. Fog clung to the air like a white parka, casting the figures in a blurry haze.¬†





 Approximately 11 meters, two potential hostiles, he couldnβÄôt make out weapons, but the way they moved indicated non-casual presence.


 


 As he turned his full attention to the two figures creeping along the sidewalk on the opposite side of the road, they turned their heads toward him, then dashed behind a parked blue minivan. One peeked out from the van, pointing something his direction.





Was that a gun?





A ghostly whisper of danger, the scent of death hung in the air βÄî tangy copper mixed with the dryness of dust. It was a taste Ash had grown accustomed to during his time overseas.¬†





His right arm shot to the Glock 10mm at his side, cold steal against his palm.





Sucking in a breath to steady himself, he took a second lookβÄî¬†





They were gone.¬†





Black mist poured out from under the minivan and evaporated into the early morning air.¬†





Shaking his head, Ash closed his eyes, counting to ten, silently as he released his breath slowly, letting it pass over tight lips.





Ash released the grip on his sidearm, which he now realized was a tape measure and not a Glock. Ash hadnβÄôt carried a weapon regularly since he left the service (for this very reason, for safety, to ensure the danger was real before he engaged).¬†





Really, Ash, come on, what danger could there possibly be in Moon-Wolf Hollow, Idaho? He thought. A random teenager shoplifting is the worst case of crime weβÄôve had in a whole year.





These days, he kept his weapons close, but locked in a case, with the ammunition stored separately, until he was at the firing range or doing a demonstrations, as he occasionally did for local organizations. In some other state, he probably wouldnβÄôt own any, but he lived in one of the last remaining hold-outs of freedom in the USA.





Ash noted that his arm was still holding the tape measure stiffly, he let his arm fall to his side, and wiped the sweat from his hands on his pant leg. Reality settled back onto his shoulders. A glimpse of himself in the glass window revealed a strong gray overtaking the sideburns. Other than a few wrinkles, he hadnβÄôt aged that badly. Some might even say he was still young.





βÄ€Hello!βÄù Oh yeah, Mrs.¬†Maverick had been asking him a question, what was it? What was she asking?





βÄ€Hello,βÄù She called again. βÄ€Ash? Is everythingβÄΠ Okay?βÄù





Ash slowly turned toward, trying to look casual. βÄ€IβÄôm sorry, Mrs.¬†Maverick,βÄù Ash said, trying to force an awkward smile into his tense jaw. βÄ€I thought I saw- βÄ€ what was he going to say that wouldnβÄôt sound crazy? βÄ€Nevermind, what was your question again?βÄù¬†





βÄ€I was asking-βÄù her lips tightened, and her nostrils flared, then she pulled at the edges of her bright yellow and orange wool blazer. βÄ€Would you be willing to do such a large custom project? Do you have the availability for that in your schedule?βÄù¬†





Ash pulled a tattered leather notebook from his messenger bag, and flipped through his notes.¬†





βÄ€Honestly,βÄù he replied. I donβÄôt know if I do at this moment. I have several other projects ahead for this next season already. But I might. Let me run some calculations back at my shop, and IβÄôll get back to you.βÄù Ash forced another smile.¬†





βÄ€Very well.βÄù She nodded curtly, and walked to a table at the other end of the cafe, then sat with a small gaggle of other wealthy bored women. Mrs Maverick was one of many such retired house wiveβÄôs that had moved to small town Idaho from some other city.





For the last 50-years or more, the town had a consistent population of 450, give or take a teenager moving away. But the last five years had seen significant growth in the larger towns in the area, and it had spread into Moon-Wolf Hollow, Idaho now. Last estimate was closer to 950, and counting.





Ash turned from his vendor table, against the window in the cafe, and checked to see if anyone else had noticed his momentary lapse of lucidity.¬†





Jenny was selling her soaps and candles at the table next to him, she was busy wrapped up in conversation with a customer, a little old lady who loved her scents.





James was pretending to organize his ironwork, for the sixtieth time that day, probably to avoid snoring in his camping chair, like he did last weekend.





Craig trimmed his pot-plants at the table on the other side, which was allowed now that Idaho finally caught up with the 21st century and realized it was supposed to be the state of Freedom and not a state where personal morals were shoved down peopleβÄôs throats. A lot of money on policing and jails was saved by foregoing the persecution of victim-less crimes, and that money was poured into increasing the availability of mental health facilities throughout the state. It was a move Ash had reservations about, but he could see the logic. And it didnβÄôt hurt in his case, as a new facility had opened in town just a few years ago, not that it was staffed at the moment. But, that was a normal small town problem. Craig had given Ash a few pointers on growing himself. He developed a unique selling pitch, by treating them as a form of Bonsai, except you could use the trimming, so to speak.





There werenβÄôt many customerβÄôs this time of day, but it was one of the last opportunities of the season, before the town buttoned up the hatches for the long winter ahead.





Handfuls of folks lingered, a tourist family staying at the motel next door eyed some crochet stuffies made to look similar to famous movie and cartoon characters, most of which Ash didnβÄôt recognize. He never had kids, and wasnβÄôt much of a kid himself in his youth.





A young man with a hoodie typed furiously at a keyboard.¬†





A woman jabbered away on the phone, failing to pacify the toddler in the stroller to her side.¬†





Two men spoke in hushed tones at a table not far from Ash.¬†





A purple and white haze turned AshβÄôs attention back to the sidewalk outside, a woman in a purple hoodie walked by just outside the window, head buried in her smartphone. She bumped into Hank, the townβÄôs crazy old homeless man who was headed the other direction, into the foggy evening. She didnβÄôt appear to notice him. He gave her a dirty look and kept walking the other way, mumbling to himself as usual.¬†





The woman sat down at a table just outside the farthest window of the Cafe, he couldnβÄôt make out her face, possibly one of the towns last tourists before the few stores left went into hibernation.¬†





An orange Maple leaf lazily floated down to the table in front her. She brushed it aside, and it fell inside a pumpkin whose top had been kicked off. The leaf caught on fire from the candle, and slowly embered away. She didnβÄôt take notice of that either. A purple and white vapor swaddled her like a blanket, as she leaned over an open book of some kind.¬†





Old Hank stared at her from the corner of the building. A small green frog sat on his shoulder. The bottom half of the frog was a mist that disappeared into HankβÄôs spine. The frogβÄôs red eyes stared at her, then at Ash, and it cocked its head to one side. Then it was gone, in a whisp of grey-green fog that continued to waft around Hank as he walked away out of sight.





Ash didnβÄôt see these creatures on every person, every time, but almost everyone had a mist cloak. Though some peopleβÄôs mists were more pronounced, while others were barely visible





The town psychic, Genevieve, called it an βÄ€auraβÄù, when he explained it to her in a rather desperate confidence one day. βÄ€Some of them must have a stronger aura,βÄù she suggested. She was a bit of a quack but he was willing to try everything to at least understand it if he couldnβÄôt get rid of it.¬†





HeβÄôd been to quite a few doctors, and that having born no fruit, he went to pastors, priests, the town psychic, a passing faith-healer, and even a local Rabbi from a larger town nearby. Each of them had their opinions and suggestions. While non of them offered any real relief, the Rabbi was the most gentle and inquisitive of the bunch, more often asking honest and curious questions, and offering few if any answers. But at least Ash felt heard and understood with the Rabbi, who had nothing to sell but time, and a good chat over coffee.





Generally, the creatures came from within a person and would go back into them, like human genie bottles. Not everyone had a creature, but people sometimes did. The Creatures werenβÄôt all alike, either. There were repeats of similar versions, but each seemed unique to the person.





βÄ€You donβÄôt fit the profile for Schizoaffective Disorder, as your life and thinking are otherwise quite normative. I would call this Post Traumatic Stress Disorder with Psychotic Features. This is rare, but can happen with serious abuse or trauma, then again, we could have a traumatic brain injury affecting the visual cortex. The mind is still a mystery to us in many waysβÄΠβÄù, his latest doctor had offered this explanation before he left the clinic without a town shrink six months ago.





The VA docs tried giving him a mountain of drugs to help him sleep and keep the delusions at a minimum. Nothing really helped, most made it worse.¬†





When the Federal government forced legalized medicinal THC on all the states as a human right, and Idaho finally gave up on the failed war on drugs and allowed doctors and people to choose their own path in life, his Idaho VA doctor said to try pot, as it was clinically shown to have better results for some people that anti-psychotics. Ash didnβÄôt bother telling the VA doc that heβÄôd already been growing it on his property for several years now.¬†





The magical medical folks at Big Pharma managed to create a pill form with less hallucinogenic properties and twice the pain killing properties, but it cost too much and his VA insurance didnβÄôt cover it yet, although the decision was before the Senate hearing committee, thanks to PTSD Health advocates. If the Fed would just finish eliminating for-profit medicine, he could try that someday. The only thing he hated more than big government was big pharma and big corporations.¬†





He liked living in one of the last small towns where people left people alone to be themselves.





For now, Ash started growing it himself a few years ago, his sleep was more regular but the delusions came and went. He could still hear the original diagnosis in his mind.





βÄ€As long as you are not a harm to yourself or others, you should be just fine. Think of it as an amusing distraction,βÄù the doctor advised.¬†





βÄî Ash heard his name.





βÄ€Ash-βÄù, Derek the BaristβÄô called his attention to the present, standing in front of his table with a frosty blending drink, and green whip cream. βÄ€Here you go, man. Extra large Java-Chip, almond milk, six shots, with peppermint and crΟ®me de menthe. I even through a few extra candied coffee beans on there for you.βÄù¬†





DerekβÄôs mists swirled around in deep blue and a streaks of orange. Ash couldnβÄôt remember the last time heβÄôd seen Derek get angry, even when some tourist was giving him a tongue lashing for being too slow.





βÄ€Thanks, DerekβÄù, Ash said as he took the drink gratefully.¬†





βÄ€Always my friend,βÄù Derek said. βÄ€HowβÄôs business been today?βÄù





βÄ€I havenβÄôt sold much, but itβÄôs nice to get out of the house a few more times before the snow makes it hard to leave the property.βÄù Ash took a sip, the cold creamy mint made his mouth tingle, βÄ€βÄΠ this is really good.βÄù¬†





He set it down. βÄ€I meanβÄΠ I can get the tractor and snow blower out and punch my way down the road when I have to. But 110 acres is a lot to manage, and my house isnβÄôt exactly off the main road.βÄù¬†





βÄ€I get. I donβÄôt know how you guys live that far out of town, my hats off to you for the effort. But I do appreciate how nice it is. Should we have another meeting up there before the roads become impassable?βÄù





βÄ€I think we can do that βÄî βÄ€ Ash stopped when he noticed Sam walk in, and waved him over to join them.¬†





βÄ€Sam,βÄù Derek gave him a bear hug, βÄ€We should have the next Kairos meeting at AshβÄôs house, before the road become impassable. I can help him get it ready for guests.βÄù





Sam smiled, but he gave Ash a sideways glance. βÄ€How are you feeling about this idea? Is it too much? Are you okay, you looked a little tense earlier, through the window.βÄù





Ash felt his gut tighten, Sam had noticed. If anyone would notice, it was Sam. He squinted at Sam, he felt the question scroll across his face, but couldnβÄôt gather the words to ask it without sounding crazy.





βÄ€I was in the jeep,βÄù Sam offered.





βÄ€Ah,βÄù Ash replied. βÄ€IβÄôm alright, I think it would be good for me.βÄù





βÄ€Okay,βÄù Sam shrugged. βÄ€You bring food, and IβÄôll bring drinks?βÄù¬†





Derek squinted at Sam, βÄ€As long as you donβÄôt bring that piss water you brought last time!βÄù¬†





Sam grinned, βÄ€IβÄôll behave and bring something a distinguished gentleman like you would approve of.βÄù





βÄ€Then I accept,βÄù Derek shook SamβÄôs hand, then AshβÄôs, and then headed back to his coffee machines.





Ash wasnβÄôt sure about all the Bible stuff, but he enjoyed having a crew around him, it reminded him of his team in the service. So he put up with the religious part for the sake of comradery, besides, most of the time they just talked about town stuff, news, or sports.





βÄ€Hey,βÄù Sam turned his full attention to Ash. βÄ€Did you hear the news?βÄù¬†





Ash picked up one of his popular Sasquatch figurines, wiggling it in front of Sam, βÄ€They finally found Big Foot?βÄù Ash noted that it felt good to smile, he didnβÄôt do that enough lately.





βÄ€No, still looking IβÄôm afraid-βÄ€ Sam still laughed at his jokes, like when they were kids. βÄ€They found a new doctor for the clinic. He just came into town last week, heβÄôs staying at the hotel until his rental cabin is set up. His moving truck comes in tomorrow and heβÄôs bringing some new equipment to town. He said heβÄôll be ready to start seeing clients by the end of week.βÄù





βÄ€ThatβÄôs good, especially with Old Hank hanging around again.βÄù Ash nodded to Old Hank still outside the window, but now barely noticeable from the corner of the building, peeping at all the people in the shop.





βÄ€Yeah, but,βÄù Sam replied, and used an over exaggerated nod back to Ash. βÄ€I think this guy might be able to help you.βÄù





βÄ€WhatβÄôs this guy got that the last fifty hasnβÄôt?βÄù Ash asked.¬†





βÄ€This guy is a bid deal or something. IβÄôm not sure where they picked him up, He says heβÄôs here to enjoy a semi-retirement or something. HeβÄôs a PhD out of Harvard or Yale or one of those big stuffy places with smart people.βÄù





βÄ€Hmm βÄîβÄ€ Ash started to form a reply, but lost it. The fog outside was creeping into his head. It had been a longer week than he planned, more sales than he hoped, which was great, but exhausting. βÄ€I guess weβÄôll see. IβÄôll check in with him after the Oktoberfest this weekend.βÄù

title: An Interview with Destiny βÄ€DeziβÄù Miller (female lead Protagonist) #WhiteNoise #Distortion #Fiction
text:
A fictional interview with my female lead Protagonist for Distortion, book 1 of the White Noise series.






As with my other article βÄ€An Interview with Dr.¬†Briar (Antagonist) #WhiteNoise #Distortion #FictionβÄù, I am using ChatGPT-4 as the Interviewer, and I am using basic Improv techniques to role-play for my fictional character.





While I had a vague idea of DeziβÄôs Want/Lie, Need/Truth that will help me set up her Character Arch, it was a vague idea. I wanted to flush out what does she really want, and why.¬†





I had to volunteer some information the interviewer wasnβÄôt asking for, because I started wondering myself, βÄ€why though?βÄù And I¬† think we have a fairly well rounded βÄ€WHYβÄù.¬†




You will find that this interview is shorter, and the answers more targeted. I already have spent years investigating this character, backstory has changed a few times, and I know who she is in a general sense, I just needed to clarify her story See more on this concept written by KM Weiland and her post on How to Write Character Arcs. Especially these sections:



PART 2: THE LIE YOUR CHARACTER BELIEVESThe Change Arc is all about the Lie Your Character Believes. His life may be horrible, or his life may seem pretty great. But festering under the surface is the Lie.¬†


PART 3: THE THING YOUR CHARACTER WANTS VS. THE THING YOUR CHARACTER NEEDSThe Lie plays out in your characterβÄôs life, and your story, through the conflict between the Thing He Needs (the Truth) and the Thing He Wants (the perceived cure for the symptoms of the Lie).¬†


PART 4: YOUR CHARACTERβÄôS GHOSTIf thereβÄôs one solid rule in fiction, itβÄôs that every effect must have a cause. If your character is in need of undergoing a change arc, then one of your first tasks is figuring out why he needs to change. What happened to him to cause him to embrace this obviously damaging Lie?

So the following interview was to help me find DeziβÄôs Want/Lie, Need/Truth.Here is the interview thread:




***





To ChatGPT-4:





Prompt: You are an interviewer, I will improv play-act a character in my novel. Her name is Destiny Miller, goes by Dezi. She is tired of doing local fluff pieceβÄôs and wants to do some real reporting on stories that matter. WeβÄôre identifying her story arch, what is her Want vs Need, Lie vs Truth, why does she want to do βÄ€realβÄù reporting so bad? Go>









ChatGPT¬†




All right, Darrell, letβÄôs dive into the character of Destiny βÄ€DeziβÄù Miller. IβÄôll start by outlining some key concepts, and you can guide the direction further if youβÄôd like more detail.¬†




Want vs.¬†Need: DeziβÄôs want is to transition from local fluff pieces to hard-hitting, impactful journalism. This desire likely stems from a need to prove her capabilities and make a significant impact in her field. However, her true need might be to find her own voice and understand what stories she genuinely values, not just what she thinks is βÄ€realβÄù reporting.¬†




Lie vs.¬†Truth: The lie Dezi might believe is that only serious, investigative journalism is worthy and that anything less is trivial. The truth could be that every story has its value and that her unique perspective, even on smaller stories, can make a significant impact.¬†




Motivation for βÄ€RealβÄù Reporting: DeziβÄôs drive for more serious reporting could stem from various factors. It might be a desire for recognition and respect in her profession, a personal connection to a larger issue, or a dissatisfaction with the superficial nature of her current assignments. Her motivation can be rooted in a past experience or a mentor who influenced her perception of journalism.¬†




Would you like to explore any of these points further, or should we delve into another aspect of DeziβÄôs character?









IβÄôll be her, you be an interviewer.¬†









Great, letβÄôs start the interview. IβÄôll be the interviewer, and youβÄôll respond as Dezi Miller.





Interviewer: βÄ€Good afternoon, Dezi. ItβÄôs a pleasure to have you here. IβÄôve noticed youβÄôve been doing a lot of local stories lately, which have been well-received. But I hear youβÄôre looking to shift gears towards more hard-hitting journalism. Can you tell us whatβÄôs driving this change in your career focus?βÄù









I donβÄôt knowβÄΠ It just seems like all I ever get assigned are silly stories about local shop openings, parades, town events, stuff like that. But I never get assigned anything serious, yaβÄô know? Like thereβÄôs all these big things happening around the area, school funds being misused, battles at the county, and our paper either covers one side of the story to the exclusion of the other, or they turn-down pieces I bring about hard hitting stuff with lame excuses, like, βÄ€WeβÄôve covered thatβÄΠβÄù, but they really havenβÄôt.¬†









Interviewer: βÄ€It sounds like youβÄôre feeling a bit frustrated with the limitations placed on your reporting. YouβÄôre seeking depth and balance in your stories. In your view, what kind of impact do you think covering these more contentious, complex issues would have on your readers and the community?βÄù









I think people really need to know whatβÄôs going on. TheyβÄôre all busy with their lives, and church events, and high school basketball games. They donβÄôt have time to sit in otherwise boring city council meetings, then loop multiple recent events together to see whatβÄôs really happening in the city. Many times IβÄôve heard people complain about how they wish theyβÄôd known something was happening after it was already too late.









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs a very insightful perspective, Dezi. It seems like youβÄôre not just looking to report news, but to provide a service to the community by connecting the dots and uncovering the bigger picture. With this in mind, what do you think has been holding you back from pursuing these more in-depth stories? Is it just the editorial direction of your current publication, or is there something else?βÄù









The County Press says theyβÄôve already covered this things in the past, but when I look, I find they havenβÄôt. IβÄôm afraid theyβÄôre actually suppressing these stories on purpose because itβÄôs bad for business, tourism, and local officials want it kept quiet.









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs a serious concern, Dezi. It sounds like youβÄôre grappling with potential editorial bias and perhaps even censorship at your current publication. Given these challenges, how do you plan to navigate this situation? Do you see yourself trying to change the system from within, or are you considering other avenues to tell these important stories?βÄù









Well thatβÄôs the issue, right? All I ever really wanted to do was to be a reporter. I finally landed a job with a real newspaper, and the only viable paper in this region. But now that IβÄôm inside, it doesnβÄôt look like they want real reporting. In fact, I think theyβÄôre actively against unbiased investigative reporting, especially the kind that inspired me to get into this in the first place.









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs quite a predicament, Dezi. ItβÄôs clear you have a passion for journalism and a strong desire to report the truth. Facing such constraints, have you considered alternative platforms or methods to pursue the kind of investigative reporting youβÄôre passionate about? Perhaps independent journalism, online platforms, or collaborations with other journalists who share your vision?βÄù









I know other options are out there, but it feels like my voice doesnβÄôt matter unless it comes from an authoritative space. Like, who cares what Dezi Miller from podunk Idaho thinks.









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs a valid concern, Dezi. Building credibility and a following can be challenging, especially outside traditional media platforms. However, your unique voice and dedication to uncovering the truth are valuable assets. Have you thought about how you might leverage your current position to gradually introduce more in-depth stories, or perhaps use your platform to highlight the need for more comprehensive coverage in a way that aligns with your publicationβÄôs interests?βÄù









I have been told in no uncertain terms, that if I publish outside of my paper, I will be let go. And this is my only income right now. Without this, IβÄôll have to go back to waiting tables, or maybe I can get a job teaching English at the high school or something. But I worked for this my whole life, how can I walk away now?









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs a tough spot to be in, Dezi. ItβÄôs a delicate balance between maintaining your livelihood and pursuing your journalistic integrity. Have you considered ways to subtly shift the narrative within your current stories to include elements of deeper, more meaningful reporting, without overtly breaking your publicationβÄôs rules? Sometimes, impactful change starts with small, strategic steps within existing boundaries.βÄù









I suppose, since The County Press is sending me to these fluff events, I could spend the time getting to know the people, the voices on the ground, get their concerns, add what I can into the pieces while gathering data for the βÄ‰realβÄô stories I think need to be told?¬†









Interviewer: βÄ€Exactly, Dezi. That sounds like a practical and strategic approach. By using your current assignments to build relationships and gather insights, youβÄôre laying the groundwork for more substantial stories. This method also allows you to maintain your position while subtly pushing the boundaries of your reporting. How do you plan to navigate the potential risks involved in this approach, especially with your editors?βÄù









IβÄôve had a full shouting match with my editor Jameson several times. IβÄôve threatened to quit on him at least twice. He really likes my writing, he says the press has had more interaction on the website and social media pages since I started. He wants me to accept that IβÄôm doing good work. ItβÄôs just not enough. So we bicker about what level of serious I can have.¬†





ItβÄôs justβÄΠ I was raised by my grandmother. My parents died when I was three. I just want it to mean something.¬†









Interviewer: βÄ€Your dedication and personal drive are truly commendable, Dezi. It sounds like you have a deep, personal connection to your work, stemming from your background and the values instilled by your grandmother. Given this, and your editorβÄôs appreciation of your writing and its impact, do you think thereβÄôs room for negotiation with Jameson? Perhaps you could propose a trial for more in-depth pieces, leveraging the increased engagement your work has brought to the publication.βÄù









HeβÄôs said many times, βÄ€Bring me something I can publish, and IβÄôll run it.βÄù But whenever I bring him anything βÄ€seriousβÄù, he turns it down. He claims he is willing to let me have some other stuff, but the only people whoβÄôs stories ever get run happen to also be good friends with the city and county elected officials.









Interviewer: βÄ€ThatβÄôs quite a challenging situation, Dezi. It seems like there might be external pressures influencing the editorial decisions at The County Press. In light of these challenges, have you thought about how you can craft stories that are both impactful and align with the publicationβÄôs apparent constraints? Perhaps finding a way to report serious issues while maintaining a balance that satisfies your editor and the publicationβÄôs network could be a key strategy.βÄù









Unfortunately, the real issues he wonβÄôt let published at all. And the remaining issues, his idea of balance is far off-center but with center sounding words.¬†









Interviewer: βÄ€ItβÄôs clear that youβÄôre in a difficult position, Dezi, where the ideals of journalism you uphold are at odds with the realities of your workplace. Considering these constraints, it might be worth exploring creative approaches. Could you consider using your platform to spotlight the voices and stories that are often overlooked, even within the parameters of less controversial topics? This way, youβÄôre still bringing attention to underrepresented perspectives, while potentially paving the way for more substantial reporting in the future.βÄù









ThatβÄôs the rock and a hard place IβÄôm in. For now, I will play the game. Grandma Miller ainβÄôt raised no fool. But I donβÄôt know how long I can ride that line, IβÄôve never been known for my patience.¬†









Interviewer: βÄ€Navigating this path with patience and strategy is indeed challenging, Dezi. But it sounds like you have a strong foundation, thanks to your grandmotherβÄôs influence. While youβÄôre βÄ‰playing the game,βÄô as you put it, keep an eye out for opportunities to pivot towards the journalism you aspire to do. Remember, every great journalist has faced challenges and had to find their unique path. Your journey is just beginning, and your determination is clear. Stay true to your values and keep seeking those openings where you can make a difference, however small they may seem at first.βÄù









ThatβÄôs what IβÄôm hoping for. My parents died in a car crash. I was supposed to be in that car with them, but I was feeling sick, so they dropped me off with Grandma Miller that night. I should have been in that car, I should be gone. But IβÄôm here. That has to mean something right? I didnβÄôt get a stay of execution just to write about Hazel Daze parades, or the grand opening of yet another mediocre restaurant concept. It has to mean something, right? I need it to mean somethingβÄΠ¬†












Interviewer: βÄ€Dezi, thatβÄôs a profound and deeply personal motivation. Your survival and the path youβÄôve chosen in journalismβÄîitβÄôs clear that you see this as more than just a career; itβÄôs a calling, a way to give meaning to your life and honor the memory of your parents. Your determination to make your work meaningful is not just commendable, itβÄôs inspiring. While the journey may be fraught with challenges and compromises, remember that every story you tell, no matter how small it seems, is a step towards that larger purpose you seek. And sometimes, the most significant impacts are made in ways we least expect. Keep holding onto that need for meaningβÄîitβÄôs a powerful driving force.βÄù









I guess soβÄΠ I mean. A business opening is meaningful to the person who is opening the business. They put their heart, passion, money, often their entire retirement or savings, into that business. They planned, often for years, and finally executed their dream. There is a story there, for sure. ThatβÄôs the story I try to tell. The human story. But when I know there are such huge things happening, things that will have ramifications on the community for decades to come, and nobody is letting the people knowβÄΠ that is a burden I just donβÄôt know if I can bear much longer. I may have to go solo, if only for those very people who are investing their lives into those bushiness that will be affected by these other things. The more I get to know those humans, the less comfortable I am waiting to tell the big stuff, because it affects them and they donβÄôt know.









Interviewer: βÄ€Your empathy and understanding of the human element in every story, whether itβÄôs a small business opening or a larger community issue, is what sets you apart, Dezi. YouβÄôre right, every story has its significance, and every decision, big or small, affects the community. ItβÄôs a heavy burden to bear, knowing that there are larger stories out there, untold and potentially impacting the very people you write about in your current assignments. Going solo is a big step, but it could be the path that allows you to report on the stories you believe in, without the constraints youβÄôre currently facing. ItβÄôs a risk, but sometimes, the biggest risks lead to the most rewarding outcomes. Whatever path you choose, itβÄôs clear that your commitment to truth and the people in your community will guide you.βÄù









Thanks George, youβÄôve always been a curious and wise monkey. I think I know what I need to do, I just have to set up the dominoes.









Interviewer: βÄ€ItβÄôs been a pleasure talking with you, Dezi. Setting up those dominoes with careful thought and strategy is key. Remember, each step you take, no matter how small, is part of a larger journey towards the kind of journalism youβÄôre passionate about. Keep your values close, and trust in your ability to make a difference. IβÄôm confident that with your determination and skill, youβÄôll find the right path. Good luck, and I look forward to seeing the impactful stories youβÄôll tell in the future.βÄù









***





I hope you enjoyed the interview,

 



Subscribe Now - Pursue a No Hiding Lifestyle¬†









 Shalom: Live Long and Prosper!

Darrell Wolfe (DG Wolfe)


Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant @¬†DarrellWolfe.com  Clifton StrengthsFinder: Intellection, Learner,¬†Ideation,¬†Achiever, Input 16Personalities (Myers-Briggs Type): INFJ

title: Grounded Cafe (take three) - #WhiteNoise #Distortion
text:
Fog hovered over the cracked asphalt just outside the window of the Grounded Cafe. AshβÄôs attention was drawn to two shadowy figures walking down the sidewalk on the opposite side of the road. He couldnβÄôt tell through the mist, but they appeared to be looking straight at him, faces hidden beneath misty charcoal hoods. He leaned in to get a better look and the figures ducked behind a blue minivan.¬†


Ice shot down AshβÄôs spine, a ghostly whisper of danger, the ever familiar shadow of death.





His right arm shot to the Glock 10mm at his side, cold steal against his palm.





Sucking in a breath to steady himself, he took a second lookβÄî They were gone. Black mist poured out from under the minivan and evaporated into the early morning air. He shook his head, closed his eyes, counting to ten, silently as he released his breath slowly over his tight lips.





Ash released the grip on his sidearm, which he now realized was a tape measure and not a Glock. Ash hadnβÄôt carried a weapon regularly since he left the service (for this very reason, for safety, to ensure the danger was real before he engaged).¬†





Really, Ash, come on, what danger could there possibly be in Moon-Wolf Hollow, Idaho? He thought. A random teenager shoplifting is the worst case of crime weβÄôve had in a whole year.


These days, he kept his weapons close, but locked in a case, with the ammunition stored separately, until he was at the firing range or doing a demonstrations, as he occasionally did for local organizations. In some other state, he probably wouldnβÄôt own any, but he lived in one of the last remaining hold-outs of freedom in the USA.





Ash noted that his arm was still holding the tape measure stiffly, he let his arm fall to his side, and wiped the sweat from his hands on his pant leg. Reality settled back onto his shoulders. A glimpse of himself in the glass window revealed a strong gray overtaking the sideburns. Other than a few wrinkles, he hadnβÄôt aged that badly. Some might even say he was still young.


Ash turned to his vendor table, against the window in the cafe. He checked to see if anyone noticed his momentary lapse of lucidity.¬†





Jenny was selling her soaps and candles at the table next to him, she was busy wrapped up in conversation with a customer, a little old lady who loved her scents.





Craig trimmed his pot-plants at the table on the other side, now that Idaho finally caught up with the 21st century and realized it was supposed to be the state of Freedom and not a state where personal morals were shoved down peopleβÄôs throats. A lot of money on policing and jails was saved by foregoing the persecution of victim-less crimes, and that money was poured into increasing the availability of mental health facilities throughout the state. It was a move Ash had reservations about, but he could see the logic. And it didnβÄôt hurt in his case, as a new facility had opened in town just a few years ago, not that it was staffed at the moment. But, that was a normal small town problem. Craig had given Ash a few pointers on growing himself.





There werenβÄôt many customerβÄôs this time of day, but it was one of the last opportunities of the season, before the town buttoned up the hatches for the long winter ahead.





A few people lingered, a tourist family staying at the motel next door was eying some crochet stuffies made to look similar to famous movie and cartoon characters.¬†





A young man with a hoodie typing furiously at a keyboard.¬†





A woman talking on the phone, failing to pacify the toddler in the stroller to her side.¬†





Two men speaking in hushed tones at a table across the way.¬†





A purple and white haze turned AshβÄôs attention to the sidewalk outside, a woman in a purple hoodie walked by just outside the window, head buried in her smartphone. She bumped into Hank, the townβÄôs crazy old homeless man who was headed the other direction. She didnβÄôt even notice. He gave her a dirty look and kept walking the other way, mumbling to himself as usual.¬†





The woman sat down at a table just outside the farthest window of the Cafe, he couldnβÄôt make out her face, possibly one of the towns last tourists before the few stores left went into hibernation.¬†


An orange Maple leaf lazily floated down to the table in front her. She brushed it aside, falling inside a pumpkin whose top had been kicked off. The leaf caught on fire from the candle inside, and slowly embered away. She didnβÄôt take notice.¬†





A purple and white vapor swaddled her like a blanket. Old Hank stared at her from the corner of the building. A small green frog sat on his shoulder. The bottom half of the frog was a mist that disappeared into HankβÄôs spine. The frogβÄôs red eyes stared at her, then at Ash, and it cocked its head to one side. Then it was gone, in a whisp of grey-green fog that continued to waft around Hank as he walked away out of sight.¬†





Ash didnβÄôt see these creatures on every person, every time. On most occasions he would see only the mists around each person, the town psychic called it an βÄ€auraβÄù, when he explained it to her in a rather desperate confidence one day. She was a bit of a quack but he tried everything to at least understand it if he couldnβÄôt get rid of it. HeβÄôd been to pastors, priests, the town psychic, a passing faith-healer, quite a few doctors, and even the local Rabbi. Each had their opinions and suggestions. While non of them offered any real relief, the Rabbi was the most gentle and inquisitive of the bunch, asking more honest and curious questions, offering few if any answers. But at least Ash felt heard with the Rabbi, who had nothing to sell but time, and a good chat over coffee.





Generally, the mists and creatures came from within each person and would go back into them, like human genie bottles. The Creatures werenβÄôt all exactly alike, either. There were repeats, but each seemed unique to the person.





βÄ€ItβÄôs called PTSD, Post Traumatic Stress DisorderβÄù, his latest doctor had said, before he left the clinic without a town shrink six months ago.





The VA docs tried giving him a mountain of drugs to help him sleep and keep the delusions at a minimum. Nothing really helped, most made it worse. When the Federal government forced legalized medicinal THC on all the states as a human right, and Idaho finally gave up on the failed war on drugs and allowed doctors and people to choose their own path in life, his Idaho VA doctor said to try it, as it was clinically shown to have better results for some people that anti-psychotics. He didnβÄôt bother telling him that heβÄôd already been growing it on his property for several years now. Maybe heβÄôd start a small boutique now that it was legal in Idaho.





The magical medical folks at Big Pharma managed to create a pill form with less hallucinogenic properties but twice the pain killing properties, but it cost too much and his VA insurance didnβÄôt cover it yet, although the decision was before the Senate hearing committee for a decision, thanks to PTSD Health advocates. If the Fed would eliminate for-profit medicine, he could try that someday.





For now, Ash started growing it himself a few years ago, his sleep was more regular but the delusions came and went. He could still hear the diagnosis in his mind.





βÄ€As long as you are not a harm to yourself or others, you should be just fine. Think of it as an amusing distraction,βÄù the doctor advised. βÄî Ash hear his name.





βÄ€Ash-βÄù, Derek the BaristβÄô called his attention to the present, standing in front of his table with a frosty blending drink, and green whip cream. βÄ€Here you go, man. Extra large Java-Chip, almond milk, three shots, with peppermint and crΟ®me de menthe. I even through a few extra candied coffee beans on there for you.βÄù¬†





βÄ€Thanks, DerekβÄù, Ash said as he took the drink gratefully.¬†





βÄ€Always my friend,βÄù Derek said. βÄ€HowβÄôs business been today?βÄù





βÄ€I havenβÄôt sold much, but itβÄôs nice to get out of the house a few more times before the snow makes it hard to leave the property. I meanβÄΠβÄù Ash took a sip, βÄ€βÄΠ this is really good.βÄù He set it down. βÄ€I meanβÄΠ I can get the tractor and snow blower out and punch my way down the road when I have to. But 110 acres is a lot to manage, and my house isnβÄôt exactly off the main road.βÄù¬†





βÄ€I get. I donβÄôt know how you guys live that far out of town, my hats off to you for the effort. But I do appreciate how nice it is. Should we have another meeting up there before the roads become impassable?βÄù





βÄ€I think we can do that βÄî βÄ€ Ash stopped when he noticed Sam walk in, he waved him over.¬†





βÄ€Sam,βÄù Derek gave him a bear hug, βÄ€We should have the next VA Support meeting at AshβÄôs house, before the road become impassable. I can help him get it ready for guests.βÄù





Sam smiled, but he gave Ash a sideways glance. βÄ€How are you feeling about this idea? Is it too much? Are you okay, you looked a little tense earlier.βÄù





Ash felt his gut tighten, Sam had noticed. If anyone would notice, it was Sam.¬†





βÄ€IβÄôm alright, I think it would be good for me.βÄù





βÄ€Okay,βÄù Sam shrugged. βÄ€You bring food, and IβÄôll bring drinks?βÄù¬†





Derek squinted at Sam, βÄ€As long as you donβÄôt bring that piss water you brought last time!βÄù¬†


Sam grinned, βÄ€IβÄôll behave and bring something a distinguished gentleman like you would approve of.βÄù





βÄ€Then I accept,βÄù Derek shook SamβÄôs hand, then AshβÄôs, and then headed back to his coffee machines.





βÄ€Hey,βÄù Sam turned his full attention to Ash. βÄ€Did you hear the news?βÄù¬†





Ash picked up one of his popular Sasquatch figurines, βÄ€They finally found Big Foot?βÄù Ash noted it felt good to smile, he didnβÄôt do that enough lately.





Sam laughed at his joke, like when they were kids. βÄ€No, still looking IβÄôm afraid. No - They found a new doctor for the clinic. He just came into town last night, heβÄôs staying at the hotel until his rental cabin is set up. His moving truck comes in tomorrow and heβÄôs bringing some new equipment to town. He said heβÄôll be ready to start seeing clients by next week.βÄù





βÄ€ThatβÄôs good, especially with Old Hank hanging around again.βÄù Ash nodded to Old Hank still outside the window, peeping around the corner at all the people in the shop.





βÄ€Yeah, but,βÄù Sam replied. βÄ€I think this guy might be able to help you.βÄù





βÄ€WhatβÄôs this guy got that the last fifty hasnβÄôt?βÄù Ash asked.¬†





βÄ€This guy is a bid deal or something. IβÄôm not sure where they picked him up, He says heβÄôs here to enjoy a semi-retirement or something. HeβÄôs a PhD out of Harvard or Yale or one of those big stuffy places with smart people.βÄù





βÄ€Hmm βÄîβÄ€ Ash started to form a reply, but lost it. The fog outside was creeping into his head. βÄ€I guess weβÄôll see. IβÄôll check in with him after the Oktoberfest.βÄù¬†




TBCβÄΠ

title: Taking Story Structure Notes
text:
IβÄôm watching videos and taking notes on writing novels, screenplays, and stories. The following are links to the various elements IβÄôve been re-learning and re-membering.







This Excel-Based Story Structure Template (link here) is based on my research and listening to multiple authors speak. Most of it is KM Weiland, but there have been elements I learned from others too.





βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî



Research (will be updating as I go):




YouTube Video:¬†21 Ways To Structure A Screenplay [WRITING MASTERCLASS]




6-Stage Plot Structure by Michael Hauge








24- Beats overlaid with the KM Weiland Story Structure
