title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
section: Understanding Variables in Python and SQL
text:
Understanding Variables in Python and SQL


Now we need to talk about what variables are (using Python & SQL):


Variables are essential in any programming or scripting language. They act as placeholders or containers for storing data values. In programming, variables enable us to write flexible and dynamic code, where we can manipulate data in various ways. LetβÄôs explore what variables are in the context of Python and SQL, two widely used languages in software development and database management.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Introduction to Python Variables


Variables are fundamental in any programming language. They allow us to store information that can be reused throughout our programs. LetβÄôs start with Python.



Variables in Python


In Python, a variable is created the moment you first assign a value to it. Variables do not need to be declared with any specific type, and they can even change type after they have been set.


A variable is essentially a name that is used to refer to some memory location in the computer, where you can store data values. You can think of it as a box where you can store something for use later in your code. Python is dynamically typed, which means you donβÄôt explicitly declare the data type of a variable. The Python interpreter infers the type based on the value assigned to the variable.


HereβÄôs a quick example:

name = "Darrell"  # String variable
age = 30  # Integer variable
is_analyst = True  # Boolean variable

In these examples: - name, age, and is_analyst are variables. - βÄ€DarrellβÄù, 30, and True are the values stored in those variables.


Python understands the type of data (string, integer, boolean) automatically.



Creating and Using Variables

# Assigning values to variables
name = "Jimmy"  # A string variable
age = 30  # An integer variable
height = 6.7  # A floating-point variable

# Using variables
print(f"{name} is {age} years old and {height} feet tall.")
## Jimmy is 30 years old and 6.7 feet tall.



Characteristics of Python Variables:




Dynamically Typed: You donβÄôt explicitly declare the data type of a variable. The Python interpreter infers the type based on the assigned value.




Mutable: The value or type of a variable can change over time.







Variables in SQL


SQL (Structured Query Language) variables are a bit different. They are used within the scope of SQL scripts, stored procedures, or queries to hold temporary data. In SQL, you typically need to declare a variable explicitly before using it, including specifying its data type.



Declaring and Using Variables in SQL


HereβÄôs how you might declare and use variables in an SQL environment (example in T-SQL, used by Microsoft SQL Server):

-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @EmployeeName VARCHAR(50);

-- Assigning a value to the variable
SET @EmployeeName = 'Jane Doe';

-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 

FROM Employees 

WHERE Name = @EmployeeName;


A more common example of a SQL variable I use when working with local government data is dates.

-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @TaxYear INT = '20240101';

-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 
FROM CadasterValues 

WHERE TaxYear = @TaxYear;



Characteristics of SQL Variables:



Explicit Declaration: Variables must be declared before use, including specifying their data type.


Scoped: The visibility and lifetime of variables are usually limited to the batch, stored procedure, or script where they are declared.


Type-Specific: When declaring a variable, you must specify its data type.





Conclusion


Variables are a fundamental aspect of both programming and scripting languages, acting as the basic units for data storage and manipulation. While Python variables are versatile and dynamically typed, SQL variables require explicit declaration and are type-specific, reflecting the structured nature of database languages. Understanding how to effectively use variables in both Python and SQL is crucial for developing flexible, efficient, and dynamic applications and queries.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî




Practice Declaring Variables


In the W3 Schools code editer, paste the following two import scripts and the first example of declaring a variable.

# Python setup for using Python in R Markdown
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np



Declaring Variables


In Python, variables do not need explicit declaration to reserve memory space. The declaration happens automatically when you assign a value to a variable.

# Assigning values to variables
x = 5
y = "Hello, World!"
print(x)
## 5
print(y)
## Hello, World!



Variable Types


Python has various data types including integers, float (decimal numbers), strings, and more. PythonβÄôs flexibility allows you to reassign variables to different data types.

# Reassigning variables to different types
x = 5.5   # x is now a float
print(x)
## 5.5
y = True  # y is now a boolean
print(y)
## True

Now play with these and come up with some of your own examples, change them and see what happns.

# Assigning values to variables
x = 100 * 5
y = "You have GOT to be kidding me!"
# There are 43,560 square feet in an acre, multiply the acres by this for the square feet
# If I have 0.56 acres, how many square feet do I have?
z = 0.56
SqFt = z * 43560

print(x)
## 500
print(y)
## You have GOT to be kidding me!
print(SqFt)
## 24393.600000000002

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 2: Introduction to SQL Variables


In SQL, variables are used differently depending on the database system. For this example, weβÄôll use T-SQL syntax, common in Microsoft SQL Server.



Declaring Variables


Variables in SQL must be declared before use. They are typically used within scripts or stored procedures.

-- Declaring a variable
DECLARE @EmployeeID int;
-- Assigning a value to the variable
SET @EmployeeID = 1;
-- Using the variable in a query
SELECT * 
FROM Employees 
WHERE EmployeeID = @EmployeeID;



Variable Types


SQL variables can be of different data types, such as INTEGER, VARCHAR (variable-length character string), and DATE. The data type of a variable dictates the kind of data it can store.

-- Declaring variables of different types
DECLARE @StartDate DATE;
DECLARE @EmployeeName VARCHAR(50);
-- Assigning values
SET @StartDate = '2021-01-01';
SET @EmployeeName = 'John Doe';

Now try this in W3 Schools

Select *
From Customers;




Note: The first column should not have a comma before, the rest do, and no comma after the last column. There are two ways to accomplish this.


Most SQL examples and tutorials will show you comma after. This works, it is the standard way. But what if I want to delete the βÄ€CountryβÄù column? Then I have to also remember to remove the comma after βÄ€CityβÄù. This isnβÄôt hard, but in long code it gets tedious as you play with the data. So I use the alternate method.

Select
CustomerID, -- No comma before CustomerID
CustomerName,
ContactName,
Address,
City,
Country -- Note how there is no comma here

From Customers;




Select alternate method:


Notice how the actual comma is the same. No comma before CustomerID, no comma after Country. But in this structure, I can delete the country line without affecting the code performance or having to remember to alter commas. Trust me, wth lots of movement in your scripts, try this method.

Select
CustomerID -- Still no comma before CustomerID
,CustomerName
,ContactName
,Address
,City
,Country -- Note how there is  still no comma here

From Customers;


Practice using a variable, though this didnβÄôt work in W3 Schools, you may have to use BigQuery or get another database access to practice SQL


DECLARE @ID VARCHAR(20) = '3';

Select
CustomerID
,CustomerName
,ContactName
,Address
,City
,Country

From Customers

Where CustomerID = @ID;

In SQL, declare a variable to hold a date and write a query to select records after this date from a table of your choice.

text:
Conclusion


Understanding variables is the first step in mastering programming. This week, weβÄôve seen how variables are used in both Python and SQL. Practice the exercises to reinforce what youβÄôve learned.


This R Markdown document template for Week 1 introduces variables in both Python and SQL, incorporating code chunks that allow for direct execution of Python and SQL code if youβÄôre using an appropriate environment that supports these languages (note that for SQL, the environment would need to be configured to connect to a SQL database). Remember to adjust the SQL syntax according to the specific SQL dialect youβÄôre using (the example uses T-SQL as an illustration).

text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Math & Logic in Python


Python provides operators to perform mathematical calculations and logical operations. LetβÄôs look at how to use these to manipulate data and control the flow of our programs.



Mathematical Operations


Python supports all basic arithmetic operations. Here are some examples:

# Mathematical operations in Python
addition = 5 + 3
subtraction = 10 - 2
multiplication = 4 * 7
division = 20 / 4
modulus = 18 % 5
print("Addition:", addition)
print("Subtraction:", subtraction)
print("Multiplication:", multiplication)
print("Division:", division)
print("Modulus:", modulus)



Logical Operations


Logical operations in Python are used to compare values. These operations return either True or False.

# Logical operations in Python
a = 5
b = 3
print("a > b:", a > b)
print("a < b:", a < b)
print("a == b:", a == b)
print("a != b:", a != b)

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 2: Math & Logic in SQL


SQL also allows for mathematical and logical operations, particularly useful in queries to filter, calculate, or manipulate data.



Mathematical Operations in SQL


SQL supports basic arithmetic operations directly in the SELECT statement or WHERE clause.


-- Example of mathematical operations in SQL
SELECT 
  EmployeeID,
  (YearlySalary / 12) AS MonthlySalary,
  (YearlySalary * 1.05) AS NextYearProjectedSalary
FROM Employees;




Logical Operations in SQL


Logical operations in SQL are primarily used in the WHERE clause to filter data based on certain conditions.

Copy code
-- Example of logical operations in SQL
SELECT * FROM Orders
WHERE OrderDate >= '2022-01-01'
AND OrderDate <= '2022-12-31';



Exercises




In Python, create a simple calculator that takes two numbers as input and performs addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.




Write a Python script that compares two variables and prints out a message based on their relation (greater than, less than, equal to).




In SQL, write a query to select all employees who have a yearly salary greater than $50,000 and have been with the company for more than 5 years.






Conclusion


Understanding and utilizing math and logic operations are fundamental in programming. This week, weβÄôve built on our foundational knowledge of variables by adding the ability to perform calculations and make decisions in both Python and SQL. Practice these operations to become more fluent in handling data within your programs.


This document provides an introductory exploration into mathematical and logical operations in Python and SQL, suited for learners advancing from basic variables to more complex expressions and data manipulation.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Input/Output in Python


PythonβÄôs input/output capabilities enable us to read from and write to files, as well as interact with users via input.



Reading User Input


Python uses the input() function to capture user input. LetβÄôs see it in action.

# Python code chunk for capturing user input
user_name = input("Enter your name: ")
print("Hello, " + user_name + "!")



Reading and Writing Files


Reading from and writing to files are common operations. HereβÄôs how to manage files in Python.

# Python code chunk for reading from and writing to a file
# Writing to a file
with open('example.txt', 'w') as file:
    file.write("Hello, Python file handling!")

# Reading from a file
with open('example.txt', 'r') as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 2: Data Manipulation in SQL


Managing data is a fundamental aspect of SQL, including retrieving, inserting, updating, and deleting records.



Retrieving Data


The SELECT statement is used to query data from a database.

-- SQL code chunk for retrieving data
SELECT FirstName, LastName FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID = 3;




Inserting Data


To add new records to a table, we use the INSERT INTO statement.

-- SQL code chunk for inserting data
INSERT INTO Employees (FirstName, LastName, DepartmentID)
VALUES ('John', 'Doe', 3);

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Exercises




Write a Python program that asks the user for their favorite number, then saves this number to a file. Read the number back from the file and print it to the console.




Create a Python script that reads a file line by line and prints each line to the console.




In SQL, write a query to insert a new record into a table of your choice, then write a query to retrieve this record.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Conclusion


Interacting with the outside world through input and output operations in Python and manipulating data in SQL are crucial for creating dynamic and responsive applications. Practice these skills to improve your ability to create more complex and interactive programs.


This document for Week 3 introduces the learners to essential I/O operations in Python and fundamental data manipulation techniques in SQL. It provides a hands-on approach with exercises designed to reinforce the concepts discussed.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Conditional Statements in Python


Conditional statements in Python enable decision-making in programs, allowing for different outcomes based on varying conditions.



if, elif, and else Statements


These statements control the flow of execution based on the truthiness of conditions.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating if, elif, and else
x = 10
if x > 5:
    print("x is greater than 5")
elif x < 5:
    print("x is less than 5")
else:
    print("x is exactly 5")

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Logical Operators


Python supports logical operators such as and, or, and not for combining conditions.

# Python code chunk for logical operators
a = True
b = False
if a and not b:
    print("a is True and b is False")

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 2: Conditional Logic in SQL


In SQL, conditional logic is often applied in queries to filter data or in the use of CASE statements for more complex conditional outcomes.



Filtering with WHERE Clause


The WHERE clause in SQL uses conditions to filter records.

-- SQL code chunk for filtering data with WHERE clause
SELECT * FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID = 3 AND Salary > 50000;

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Using CASE Statements


The CASE statement in SQL allows for conditional logic in query results.

-- SQL code chunk for CASE statements
SELECT EmployeeID, FirstName, LastName,
    CASE 
        WHEN Salary > 60000 THEN 'High'
        WHEN Salary BETWEEN 40000 AND 60000 THEN 'Medium'
        ELSE 'Low'
    END AS SalaryRange
FROM Employees;

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Exercises



Write a Python program that asks the user to enter a number. Use conditional statements to print whether the number is positive, negative, or zero.


Create a Python script that uses logical operators to check multiple conditions and outputs a result based on these conditions.


In SQL, write a query that uses a CASE statement to categorize employees by their salary ranges (e.g., Low, Medium, High).

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Conclusion


Conditional statements are fundamental to programming, enabling dynamic decision-making based on different conditions. By mastering conditionals in both Python and SQL, you can write more flexible and powerful code that responds to various scenarios effectively.


This Week 4 document offers a comprehensive guide on implementing conditional logic in Python and SQL. ItβÄôs designed to build on the foundational skills taught in previous weeks, gradually increasing the learnerβÄôs ability to handle more complex programming tasks.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Loops in Python


Loops in Python are powerful, allowing you to iterate over items of any sequence including lists, strings, or even ranges of numbers.



for Loops


The for loop in Python is used to iterate over elements of a sequence.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating a for loop
for i in range(5):
    print("Python iteration number:", i)
## Python iteration number: 0
## Python iteration number: 1
## Python iteration number: 2
## Python iteration number: 3
## Python iteration number: 4



while Loops


The while loop executes as long as the provided condition is true.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating a while loop
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print("While loop count:", count)
    count += 1

Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Section 1: Functions in Python


Functions in Python are defined using the def keyword and can accept parameters and return values.



Defining and Calling Functions


HereβÄôs how to define a simple function in Python that takes an argument and returns a value.

# Python code chunk for defining and calling a function
def greet(name):
    return "Hello, " + name + "!"

# Calling the function
print(greet("Darrell"))
## Hello, Darrell!

Parameters and Return Values Functions can have multiple parameters and return values. They can also return multiple values in the form of tuples.

# Python code chunk for a function with multiple parameters and return values
def arithmetic_operations(a, b):
    return a+b, a-b, a*b, a/b

# Calling the function and unpacking return values
addition, subtraction, multiplication, division = arithmetic_operations(10, 5)
print("Addition:", addition)
print("Subtraction:", subtraction)
print("Multiplication:", multiplication)
print("Division:", division)

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
section: Section 2: Stored Procedures and Functions in SQL
text:
Section 2: Stored Procedures and Functions in SQL


Stored procedures and functions in SQL allow for encapsulating SQL queries and commands for reuse and execution.



Creating and Using a Stored Procedure


Stored procedures can perform operations such as data manipulation and return the results.

-- SQL code chunk for creating and using a stored procedure (T-SQL syntax)
CREATE PROCEDURE GetEmployeeInfo
    @EmployeeID INT
AS
BEGIN
    SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE EmployeeID = @EmployeeID;
END;
GO

-- Invoking the stored procedure
EXEC GetEmployeeInfo @EmployeeID = 1;




Creating and Using a Function in SQL


Functions in SQL are used to compute values and cannot change the database state.

-- SQL code chunk for creating and using a function (T-SQL syntax)
CREATE FUNCTION GetFullName (@FirstName VARCHAR(100), @LastName VARCHAR(100))
RETURNS VARCHAR(200)
AS
BEGIN
    RETURN @FirstName + ' ' + @LastName;
END;
GO

-- Using the function in a SELECT statement
SELECT dbo.GetFullName(FirstName, LastName) AS FullName FROM Employees;




Exercises


Create a Python function that calculates the factorial of a number and test it with different values. Write a Python function that checks whether a given word is a palindrome (reads the same backward and forward).


In SQL, create a stored procedure that retrieves employees by their department ID and test it. Develop an SQL function that calculates the yearly salary increase based on current salary and percentage increase rate.




Conclusion


Functions and stored procedures are crucial for writing clean, efficient, and reusable code. By encapsulating specific tasks into these constructs, you can simplify complex operations in both Python and SQL, making your code more organized and easier to maintain.


This Week 6 document aims to introduce and solidify the understanding and usage of functions in Python and stored procedures and functions in SQL, through both explanation and hands-on practice with code examples.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
section: Section 1: Sequences and Data Structures in Python
text:
Section 1: Sequences and Data Structures in Python


Python offers a variety of data structures for efficiently storing and managing data. LetβÄôs explore lists, dictionaries, and sets.



Lists


Lists in Python are ordered sequences that can hold a variety of object types. They support indexing, slicing, and a host of methods for manipulation.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating lists
my_list = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(my_list[0])  # Accessing the first element
## 1
my_list.append(6)  # Adding an element
print(my_list)
## [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]



Dictionaries


Dictionaries store key-value pairs and are ideal for fast lookup of values by key.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating dictionaries
my_dict = {'name': 'Darrell', 'age': 30}
print(my_dict['name'])  # Accessing value by key
my_dict['profession'] = 'Data Analyst'  # Adding a new key-value pair
print(my_dict)



Sets


Sets are unordered collections of unique elements, useful for operations like union, intersection, and difference.

# Python code chunk for demonstrating sets
my_set = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
print(my_set)
my_set.add(6)  # Adding a new element
print(my_set)

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
section: Section 2: Relational Data Model and SQL Operations
text:
Section 2: Relational Data Model and SQL Operations


The relational data model organizes data into tables (relations) that relate to each other through foreign keys.



JOIN Operations


JOIN operations in SQL allow for the combination of rows from two or more tables based on a related column between them.

-- SQL code chunk for demonstrating JOIN operations
SELECT Employees.Name, Departments.Name
FROM Employees
JOIN Departments ON Employees.DepartmentID = Departments.ID;




Subqueries


Subqueries are queries within queries, useful for complex data retrieval.

-- SQL code chunk for demonstrating subqueries
SELECT Name
FROM Employees
WHERE DepartmentID IN (SELECT ID FROM Departments WHERE Name = 'IT');




Exercises



Create a Python script that uses lists to store the names of your favorite movies and then prints them out.


Write a Python program that uses dictionaries to store information about a book (title, author, year), then prints out the information.


Use sets in Python to find the common elements between two sets of numbers.


Write an SQL query using JOIN to retrieve employee names and their department names.


Craft an SQL query with a subquery to find all employees in a specific department based on the department name.





Conclusion


Understanding and utilizing sequences and data structures in Python and grasping the relational data model and its operations in SQL are pivotal for managing complex data. These constructs enhance your ability to organize, access, and manipulate data effectively, serving as fundamental skills in both programming and database


This Week 7 document is crafted to enhance the learnerβÄôs ability to work with complex data structures in Python and understand relational database operations in SQL, bridging the gap between basic programming concepts and more advanced data manipulation techniques.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text: βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄî

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Project Objective


The goal of this project is to create a Python application that performs CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations on a SQL database. This application will serve as a demonstration of your ability to apply programming concepts in Python and SQL to solve real-world problems.



Project Requirements



Python Application:


Use variables, loops, and conditional statements to navigate the program flow.


Implement functions to organize your code into logical blocks.


Utilize lists or dictionaries to manage data within the application.




SQL Database:


Design a simple database schema with at least one table to store data.


Use SQL commands to create the table and define its structure.


Perform insert, select, update, and delete operations from your Python application.







Suggested Project Ideas



Contact Book Application: A simple application to store, retrieve, update, and delete contact information (e.g., name, phone number, email) in a database.


Inventory Management System: An application to manage products in an inventory, including adding new products, updating stock levels, retrieving product information, and deleting products from the inventory.

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Implementation Guide



Setting Up the Database


First, design your database schema and create your tables using SQL.

-- Example SQL code for creating a table
CREATE TABLE Contacts (
    ContactID INT PRIMARY KEY,
    Name VARCHAR(100),
    PhoneNumber VARCHAR(15),
    Email VARCHAR(100)
);

title: Learning Coding Fundamentals with Python and SQL
text:
Developing the Python Application


Outline the structure of your Python application, defining the functions needed for each operation.



Connecting to the Database


Use a Python library such as sqlite3 or pyodbc to connect to your SQL database.

# Python code chunk for connecting to a database
import sqlite3

# Connect to SQLite database (or change to connect to another database type)
conn = sqlite3.connect('mydatabase.db')



CRUD Operations


Implement functions for each CRUD operation. For example, creating a new contact:

# Python code chunk for inserting data into the database
def add_contact(conn, contact_info):
    sql = ''' INSERT INTO Contacts(Name, PhoneNumber, Email)
              VALUES(?,?,?) '''
    cur = conn.cursor()
    cur.execute(sql, contact_info)
    conn.commit()



Exercises


Plan the database schema for your project idea. What tables and fields will you need? Outline the Python functions you will implement to interact with the database. Begin coding your Python application, starting with the database connection and a simple interface for performing the CRUD operations.




Conclusion


This final weekβÄôs project is an opportunity to apply everything youβÄôve learned throughout this course. By building a Python application that interacts with a SQL database, you demonstrate not only your understanding of coding principles but also your ability to apply these concepts to create functional, real-world applications. Good luck, and have fun with your project!


This Week 8 document is designed to guide you through the process of applying the coding fundamentals youβÄôve learned into a cohesive project, emphasizing the practical application of programming concepts in a real-world scenario.

title: I canβÄôt find my Blogger.com DNS record?! HereβÄôs how to find it. It took me a long time because GoogleβÄôs own instructions fail.
text:
I use Blogger.com for my websites. I find it easier to use without having to know a lot of technical things.¬†However, I bought my domain names from a third-party website, and I host them on Blogger.com.¬†After years of this, I tried a hosted Word Press site, I found the GUI awful and editor even worse. I'm sure it has amazing features and it looked pretty, but it was absolutely useless to me as a writer.So... I went back to Blogger.com, but ran into an odd issue. I needed my personal DNS record to provide to my domain provider.¬†The Google instructions "Set up a custom domain" say that I should get a pop-up message with my DNS records. There is noplace in the blogger interface to find the DNS record that I can find, neither in the website or elsewhere. That is an odd user interface failure. Others expressed the same issue and even ChatGPT4o Pro couldn't help, it kept taking me back to these instructions.¬†Finally! I found the answer on this page:Why I'm not getting CNAME records from Blogger?But even that instruction was slightly confusing, so I decided to provide some screenshots so someone can find the answer faster.¬†1. Go to: the new Google Search Console
https://www.google.com/webmasters/verification

2. From the top left, click the dropdown tool and select the Blogger.com domain you want to use for your new domain name. Typically, this will look like yournamehere.blogspot.com.3. From the bottom left, click on Settings4. In the middle of this page you should see General settings, and just under that the first block reads: Ownership verification5. Click Ownership verification, if you are already the verified owner (which is likely if you're getting this missing DNS issue), you should see a green circle and "You are a verified owner".6. Click the bottom block that reads Domain name provider > Associate a DNS record with Google, click the light grey arrow. You should see a result that looks like this:Domain name provider | Associate a DNS record with Google1 Select record type:2 Sign in to your domain name provider (e.g. godaddy.com or namecheap.com)3 Copy the TXT record below into the DNS configuration for darrellglenwolfe.blogspot.comgoogle-site-verification=xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx4 Press verify belowNote: DNS changes may take some time to apply. If Search Console doesnΟΔβ²§β³Δt find the record immediately, wait a day and then try to verify again. Learn more¬†* The xxx would be replaced by your personal identification.

Shalom¬†Ο½¬©Ο•¬ΗΟ½¬¹Ο½≈™Ο½βÄΔΟ•¬ΙΟ½¬ù: Live Long and Prosper!

Darrell Wolfe

Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant | Freelancer

title: Lesson Plan: Acquiring a Business Using SBA 7(a) Loans - Overview
text:
ChatGPT-4o Prompt: After a multipronged conversation with GPT about SBA loans, I asked:¬†Draft up a lesson plan based on this entire conversation on all the things I would need to learn and what places I should go to try to learn them.


Lesson Plan: Acquiring a Business Using SBA 7(a) Loans


Objective:


To understand the process and requirements for acquiring a business using SBA 7(a) loans and to identify the resources needed to successfully navigate this process.



Week 1: Introduction and Preliminary Research


Objectives:



Understand the basics of SBA 7(a) loans.


Identify the steps involved in acquiring a business.



Activities:




Research SBA 7(a) Loans:



Read articles and guides on the SBA website about the 7(a) loan program.


Watch educational videos on YouTube about SBA loans.





Familiarize with the Business Acquisition Process:



Review resources on SCORE.org about buying a business.


Read case studies of successful business acquisitions.





Resources:



SBA.gov


SCORE.org


YouTube (search for SBA 7(a) loan tutorials)




Week 2: Identifying Potential Businesses and Financial Viability


Objectives:



Learn how to identify businesses for sale.


Understand financial statements and business valuation.



Activities:




Identifying Businesses:



Explore online marketplaces like BizBuySell to find businesses for sale.


Network with local business owners and attend business networking events.





Financial Analysis:



Take online courses or read guides on understanding financial statements.


Learn the basics of business valuation from resources like Investopedia.





Resources:



BizBuySell.com


Local business networking events


Online courses (Coursera, Udemy)




Week 3: Creating a Business Plan


Objectives:



Understand the components of a business plan.


Develop a draft business plan for acquiring a business.



Activities:




Business Plan Creation:



Use SCOREβÄôs business plan templates and guides.


Attend a SCORE workshop or webinar on business planning.





Drafting Your Plan:



Write a draft business plan, focusing on your strategy for running and growing the business you plan to acquire.


Seek feedback from a SCORE mentor.





Resources:



SCORE.org (business plan templates and workshops)


Local SCORE chapter for mentoring




Week 4: Securing Financing


Objectives:



Learn how to approach lenders and secure SBA 7(a) loan financing.


Understand the documentation required for loan applications.



Activities:




Approaching Lenders:



Identify local banks and credit unions that offer SBA 7(a) loans.


Learn how to present your business plan and financials to lenders.





Loan Documentation:



Gather necessary documentation such as personal financial statements, tax returns, and business financials.


Attend a webinar or workshop on SBA loan applications.





Resources:



Local banks and credit unions


SBA.gov (loan application guides)


SCORE workshops




Week 5: Negotiating and Closing the Deal


Objectives:



Understand the negotiation process for acquiring a business.


Learn the legal and financial steps to finalize the purchase.



Activities:




Negotiation Techniques:



Read books or take online courses on negotiation.


Role-play negotiation scenarios with a mentor or peer.





Closing the Deal:



Consult with a business attorney to understand legal requirements.


Prepare for the closing process by reviewing checklists and guides.





Resources:



Books on negotiation (e.g., βÄ€Getting to YesβÄù by Fisher and Ury)


Online courses (Coursera, Udemy)


Local business attorney




Week 6: Transition and Management


Objectives:



Plan for a smooth transition of business ownership.


Develop management skills for running the acquired business.



Activities:




Transition Planning:



Create a transition plan detailing how you will take over operations.


Communicate with employees, customers, and suppliers about the change in ownership.





Management Skills:



Take courses on small business management.


Join local business groups or networks for ongoing support.





Resources:



Online courses (Coursera, Udemy)


Local business groups (Chamber of Commerce)




Ongoing: Mentorship and Continuous Learning


Objectives:



Maintain ongoing support and learning through mentorship.


Continuously improve business management skills.



Activities:




Mentorship:



Regularly meet with a SCORE mentor for advice and support.


Join a mastermind group or business support group.





Continuous Learning:



Stay updated with the latest business trends and best practices.


Attend workshops, webinars, and conferences.





Resources:



SCORE.org (mentorship)


Local business support groups


Industry conferences and workshops

title: Goal: Analyze real tangible differences in quality of life for American citizens
text:
Goal: Analyze real tangible differences in quality of life for American citizens








QUESTION ONE





ChatGPT o1 Prompt:







I want to analyze real tangible differences in quality of life for American citizens against government policies, economic conditions, healthcare system, prison system, mental health systems. There are no simple answers. I could also see how an analyze could get so convoluted as to be unhelpful. I have also learned that when it comes to data, we should ask smaller questions and then compiles the results of a lot of smaller questions, rather than trying to grab one sweeping dataset. That being said, before we worry about the status of systems or policies, letβÄôs consider what datasets could serve as indicators of quality of life, extrapolating out over decades?¬†¬†




Write this same answer but with links to each of those sites









ChatGPT o1 Answer:






Analyzing the quality of life over decades requires a multifaceted approach that incorporates various indicators reflecting the well-being of American citizens. To assess tangible differences in quality of life and understand how government policies, economic conditions, healthcare, the prison system, and mental health systems impact it, you can rely on a combination of datasets. Below are key datasets and indicators that can serve as proxies for quality of life, along with suggestions on how to use them effectively, including links to the relevant resources.



1. Life Expectancy and Mortality Rates




Datasets:



National Vital Statistics Reports by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)


CDC National Vital Statistics Reports




World Bank Life Expectancy Data


World Bank Life Expectancy Data







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Life expectancy is a fundamental indicator of a populationβÄôs overall health and well-being.


Mortality rates by cause can reveal trends in public health issues.





Usage:



Analyze trends in life expectancy over decades.


Correlate changes with healthcare policies, economic shifts, or major events (e.g., pandemics).






2. Health Outcomes and Access to Healthcare




Datasets:



Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) by CDC


CDC BRFSS




National Health Interview Survey (NHIS)


CDC NHIS




Healthcare Expenditure Data from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS)


CMS National Health Expenditure Data




Kaiser Family Foundation Health Statistics


KFF Health Costs







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Health outcomes reflect the effectiveness of the healthcare system and access to medical services.


Chronic disease prevalence and mental health statistics indicate the populationβÄôs health status.





Usage:



Examine trends in chronic illnesses, mental health issues, and healthcare access.


Assess the impact of healthcare reforms like the Affordable Care Act on health outcomes.






3. Economic Indicators




Datasets:



Gross Domestic Product (GDP) per Capita from the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA)


BEA GDP Data




Unemployment Rates from the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS)


BLS Unemployment Data




Poverty Rates from the U.S. Census Bureau


Census Bureau Poverty Data




Consumer Price Index (CPI) for inflation adjustment


BLS CPI Data







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Economic stability is closely linked to quality of life.


Employment and income levels affect access to resources and services.





Usage:



Track economic growth, unemployment, and poverty over time.


Adjust for inflation to compare real income changes.






4. Income Inequality Measures




Datasets:



Gini Coefficient Data from the U.S. Census Bureau


Census Bureau Gini Index




Income Distribution Tables (percentiles, quintiles)


Census Bureau Income Data







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Income inequality can affect social cohesion and access to opportunities.


Understanding the distribution of wealth helps in assessing economic disparities.





Usage:



Analyze changes in income inequality over decades.


Correlate with tax policies, minimum wage laws, and social welfare programs.






5. Educational Attainment




Datasets:



National Center for Education Statistics (NCES)


NCES Data and Tools




High School and College Graduation Rates from the Department of Education


DOE Graduation Rates




Program for International Student Assessment (PISA) scores


PISA Results







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Education levels influence employment opportunities and economic mobility.


Literacy and numeracy skills are foundational for personal and societal development.





Usage:



Examine trends in educational attainment and quality.


Assess the impact of education policies like No Child Left Behind or Common Core.






6. Crime Rates and Incarceration




Datasets:



Uniform Crime Reporting (UCR) Program by the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)


FBI UCR Program




Bureau of Justice Statistics (BJS) for incarceration data


BJS Corrections Statistics




National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) program


BJS NPS Data







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Crime rates affect community safety and trust in institutions.


Incarceration rates can reflect on the justice system and social policies.





Usage:



Analyze trends in violent and property crimes.


Examine the effects of policies like the War on Drugs on incarceration rates.






7. Mental Health Statistics




Datasets:



National Survey on Drug Use and Health (NSDUH)


SAMHSA NSDUH Data




Suicide Rates from the CDCβÄôs National Center for Health Statistics


CDC Suicide Data




Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) data


SAMHSA Data







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Mental health is integral to overall quality of life.


Prevalence of mental health disorders indicates societal stressors.





Usage:



Track changes in mental health diagnoses and treatment access.


Correlate with economic downturns, social media usage, or healthcare policies.






8. Social Mobility and Economic Opportunity




Datasets:



Opportunity Insights by Harvard University (research by Raj Chetty)


Opportunity Insights Data Library




Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID)


PSID Main Page







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Social mobility reflects the ability to improve oneβÄôs economic status.


Indicates the effectiveness of education and employment policies.





Usage:



Analyze intergenerational income changes.


Assess the impact of education funding and job training programs.






9. Housing Affordability




Datasets:



Housing Affordability Index from the National Association of Realtors


NAR Housing Affordability Index




American Housing Survey (AHS) by the U.S. Census Bureau


Census Bureau AHS




Housing Vacancy Survey

Housing costs are a significant part of living expenses.


Homeownership rates can indicate economic stability.





Usage:



Examine trends in housing prices relative to income.


Assess the impact of housing policies and market fluctuations.






10. Environmental Quality




Datasets:



Air Quality Index (AQI) data from the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)


EPA Air Quality Data




Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) Program


EPA TRI Data

Environmental conditions affect health and quality of life.


Pollution can lead to respiratory issues and other health problems.

Track changes in air and water quality.


Correlate with environmental regulations and industrial activities.






11. Work-Life Balance and Labor Conditions




Datasets:

Analyze trends in working hours, vacation time, and job satisfaction.

12. Subjective Well-Being Measures




Datasets:



Gallup World Poll

World Happiness Report Data




General Social Survey (GSS)


GSS Data Explorer

Provides insights beyond economic and health indicators.





Usage:



Incorporate subjective well-being into overall quality of life assessments.


Compare with objective indicators to identify discrepancies.






13. Access to Technology




Datasets:



Internet and Broadband Access Data from the Federal Communications Commission (FCC)


FCC Broadband Deployment Data




Pew Research CenterβÄôs Technology Surveys


Pew Internet & Technology







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Technology access influences education, employment, and social connectivity.


Digital divide can exacerbate inequalities.





Usage:



Assess how internet access has expanded over time.


Examine the impact of technology on remote work and education.






14. Government Policy Data




Datasets:



Historical Legislative Data from Congress.gov


Congress.gov Legislative Information




Policy Implementation Dates from federal and state government archives


Federal Register


State Legislative Websites (varies by state)







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Understanding when policies were enacted helps in correlating with changes in quality of life indicators.


Policy analysis provides context for data trends.





Usage:



Map significant policy changes to shifts in your chosen indicators.


Analyze the short-term and long-term effects of legislation.






15. Demographic Data




Datasets:



U.S. Census Data


U.S. Census Bureau Data




American Community Survey (ACS)


Census Bureau ACS




Population Estimates Program


Census Bureau Population Estimates







Why ItβÄôs Useful:



Demographics influence and contextualize quality of life data.


Allows for analysis of subpopulations and identification of disparities.





Usage:



Control for age, race, gender, and other demographic variables.


Identify trends within specific groups or regions.






Approach to Analysis




Define Specific Questions:



Start with focused questions, such as βÄ€How has access to healthcare affected life expectancy over the past 40 years?βÄù


Break down the larger inquiry into manageable segments.





Collect and Compile Data:



Gather data for each indicator from reliable sources.


Ensure datasets are comparable over time (consistent methodologies).





Adjust for Inflation and Population Changes:



Use CPI to adjust economic data for inflation.


Account for population growth or decline in per capita analyses.





Use Statistical Methods:



Apply regression analysis to identify correlations and potential causations.


Control for confounding variables to isolate the effects of interest.





Correlate with Policy Changes:



Align data timelines with the implementation of major policies.


Consider lag effects where policies may take time to impact indicators.





Visualize Data:



Create charts and graphs to illustrate trends and relationships.


Use GIS mapping for geographical analyses.





Interpret Findings:



Look for patterns and anomalies.


Consider historical events that may have influenced the data.





Compile Results:



Summarize findings from smaller questions to build a comprehensive picture.


Discuss how different indicators interact and affect overall quality of life.






Additional Resources



National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER):


NBER Data




Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS):


IPUMS USA




HealthData.gov:


HealthData.gov




OECD Data:


OECD Statistics






Final Considerations



Interdisciplinary Approach: Quality of life is multidimensional; integrating data from health, economics, sociology, and environmental studies will provide a more holistic understanding.


Data Limitations: Be aware of potential gaps or inconsistencies in historical data.


Ethical Analysis: Consider the ethical implications of data interpretation, especially when dealing with sensitive topics like health and incarceration.




By focusing on these datasets and following a structured approach, you can effectively analyze the tangible differences in quality of life for American citizens over decades. This method allows you to build a comprehensive analysis that considers the complex interplay between various societal factors and government policies.



Note: Access to some datasets may require registration or subscription. Ensure you comply with the terms of use for each data source.


  

QUESTION TWO



ChatGPT o1 Prompt:





What analysis has been done by reputable data analysts (with as little bias as possible) around which US Party policies have what impacts on the quality of life of US Citizens?








ChatGPT o1 Answer:






Reputable data analysts and research institutions have conducted numerous studies to assess how policies enacted by the U.S. Democratic and Republican parties impact the quality of life for American citizens. These analyses cover a wide range of policy areas, including economics, healthcare, education, environmental regulation, and social justice. Below is a synthesis of key findings from these studies, aiming to present the information as objectively as possible.



1. Economic Policies




Income Inequality and Taxation



Findings: Analyses by the Tax Policy Center indicate that tax cuts implemented under Republican administrations often disproportionately benefit higher-income individuals, potentially exacerbating income inequality. Democratic policies typically advocate for progressive taxation aimed at redistributing wealth more equitably.


Impact on Quality of Life: Increased income inequality can lead to reduced economic mobility and access to resources for lower-income groups.





Employment and Job Growth



Findings: Data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics suggest that job growth rates have varied under different administrations, but attributing these changes solely to party policies is challenging due to external factors like global economic conditions.


Impact on Quality of Life: Steady employment rates contribute to economic stability and personal well-being.





2. Healthcare Policies




Affordable Care Act (ACA)



Findings: Enacted under the Obama administration, the ACA significantly reduced the uninsured rate, especially among low-income individuals and minorities, according to the Kaiser Family Foundation.


Impact on Quality of Life: Improved access to healthcare services leads to better health outcomes and reduced financial strain from medical expenses.





Medicaid Expansion



Findings: States that expanded Medicaid saw reductions in mortality rates and improved overall health outcomes, as reported in the New England Journal of Medicine.


Impact on Quality of Life: Expanded coverage increases preventive care and reduces chronic disease complications.





3. Education Policies




Public Education Funding



Findings: Democratic policies often support increased funding for public education. The National Bureau of Economic Research found that increased investment correlates with better educational outcomes.


Impact on Quality of Life: Higher educational attainment is linked to improved employment opportunities and socioeconomic status.





School Choice and Vouchers



Findings: Republican policies frequently promote school choice and voucher programs. Studies show mixed results, with some improvements in student satisfaction but inconsistent academic performance.


Impact on Quality of Life: School choice can empower parents but may divert funds from public schools, affecting resource availability.





4. Environmental Policies




Climate Change and Regulation



Findings: Democratic policies generally advocate for stronger environmental regulations to combat climate change. The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) reports that such regulations can lead to improved air and water quality.


Impact on Quality of Life: Better environmental quality reduces health risks associated with pollution and contributes to overall public health.





Energy Policies



Findings: Republican policies often prioritize energy independence through fossil fuels. While this can boost certain economic sectors, it may have adverse environmental effects.


Impact on Quality of Life: Short-term economic gains may be offset by long-term environmental and health costs.





5. Social Policies




Civil Rights and Equality



Findings: Democratic initiatives frequently focus on expanding civil rights protections. Organizations like the Human Rights Campaign note progress in LGBTQ+ rights and anti-discrimination laws under Democratic leadership.


Impact on Quality of Life: Enhanced protections contribute to social inclusion and mental well-being for marginalized groups.





Criminal Justice Reform



Findings: Both parties have engaged in criminal justice reform, though approaches differ. The Brennan Center for Justice highlights bipartisan efforts that have begun addressing mass incarceration.


Impact on Quality of Life: Reforms can lead to reduced incarceration rates and better reintegration opportunities for formerly incarcerated individuals.





6. Economic Mobility and Social Welfare




Social Safety Nets



Findings: Democratic policies typically support robust social safety nets. Research by economists like Raj Chetty shows that areas with strong social services have higher rates of economic mobility.


Impact on Quality of Life: Access to social programs can mitigate poverty and provide pathways to upward mobility.





Regulatory Policies



Findings: Republican policies often favor deregulation to stimulate business growth. While this can enhance economic activity, it may also reduce consumer protections.


Impact on Quality of Life: Deregulation can lower costs but might increase risks related to safety, finance, and the environment.





7. Fiscal Responsibility



Budget Deficits and National Debt


Findings: Analyses by the Congressional Budget Office (CBO) reveal that both parties have contributed to increasing national debt through various policies, including tax cuts and increased spending.


Impact on Quality of Life: High national debt can affect long-term economic stability and burden future generations.






Considerations and Limitations



Complex Attribution: Directly linking policy outcomes to party initiatives is complex due to confounding variables like global events, technological advancements, and socio-cultural shifts.


Data Bias: While efforts are made to reduce bias, some studies may reflect the perspectives of the organizations conducting them.


Dynamic Policies: Party platforms evolve, and bipartisan cooperation on certain issues can blur distinctions.




Conclusion


Studies by reputable analysts suggest that:




Democratic Policies tend to focus on social welfare, healthcare access, education funding, environmental protection, and civil rights. These policies are associated with improvements in public health, educational outcomes, and social equity.




Republican Policies often emphasize economic growth through deregulation, tax cuts, and reduced government spending. Proponents argue that these strategies can lead to increased economic opportunities and personal freedoms.




Ultimately, the impact of party policies on the quality of life is multifaceted and depends on individual values, socioeconomic status, and specific community needs. For the most nuanced understanding, it is advisable to consult a range of studies and consider the methodologies and contexts in which they were conducted.



Note: This summary is based on information available up to October 2023. For the most recent analyses, please refer to the latest reports from the mentioned organizations and research institutions.



    Shalom¬†Ή©÷ΗΉ¹Ή€Ήï÷ΙΉù: Live Long and Prosper!

Darrell Wolfe

Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant | Freelancer

title: Writing
text:
This writing hub separates personal, biblical studies, and creative writing into distinct pages while keeping search unified across the whole website.


Creative Writing
I write speculative fiction at the intersection of fantasy, science fiction, and the uncanny, with a deep interest in meaning, identity, and transformation. This page is the central home for my creative writing identity and the stories growing out of it.


Biblical Studies
I am a biblical studies major and I write about the Bible by dragging it out of modern Churchianity and back into its ancient world, reading it within its Ancient Near East (ANE) and Second Temple Jewish contexts, and asking the kinds of questions that polished religion usually tries to avoid. IβÄôm especially interested in the BibleβÄôs worldview, its systems of meaning, and the way it shapes identity, perception, and power. I have little interest in church tradition, church fathers, or what your pastor thinks the Bible says. The biblical authors had their own worldview, their own questions, and their own answers, and I want to understand those on their own terms. This page is for biblical studies rooted in context, worldview, close reading, and the kind of questions polished religion usually tries to avoid.


Data Nerd
By day, I wrangle data for a living. This page is where I write about SQL, dashboards, automation, reporting, and the deeply satisfying work of forcing chaotic information to sit down, behave, and tell the truth.


Personal
This page is for the randomness in my head that refuses to fit neatly into the other categories: reflections, rabbit trails, rants, weird connections, and the occasional thought that showed up uninvited but made a compelling case for itself.


Use the search box in the top navigation to find content across every section.

title: Transformation Ministry
text:
Today I had the honor of meeting a group called, Heart Writers. ItβÄôs a group for anyone who feels they have a book to write.¬†There were poets, short story writers, fiction writers, memoir writers, and others.  During our talks, the leader presented the idea that all of our writing, even the seemingly random blog posts, could be a form of ministry. In my mind, writing and ministry mean theological works such as explaining the Bible. IβÄôve done plenty of that; however, that got me thinking about Ted Dekker and his teaching on writing fiction.




He said that one major key is for your character to undergo a major internal transformation.  Ted Dekker said (see more here):



What is story? Story is a series of events involving worthy characters who change (are transformed) as a result of those events.

This was a great reminder that ministry means a lot of things. As TedβÄôs character changes, IβÄôm going through that transformation with him/her. IβÄôm being transformed a little in the process myself. That is TedβÄôs Ministry to me, the reader. That is my ministry to my readers, few and far between though they may be.  Long after the average reader had forgotten all ten steps, and most of the habits, he/she will remember Frodo almost quitting and Sam coming to his aide, to push him to move on. They will feel with Captain America as he refuses to fight his best friend. They will laugh and cry with the revelation that God can be a black woman (The Shack).  Story is what God used to reach us on the Bible. There are facts, lists, but the vast majority of GodβÄôs Word to Mankind was told through story. Jesus said, βÄ€greater things that I doβÄù. He was a master storyteller above all else he did. Audiences may have gotten healed when they came to him, but they stayed long enough to get healed because they were captivated by his stories.





Story is the most important ministry I can be involved in, for my skill set.  I NEED to tell a storyβÄΠ Maybe more than one. 

Darrell G. Wolfe








Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant |¬†Multipotentialite

title: SIN, sins, and the Promises of God
text:
Recently, I saw this statement (as a snippet of a much larger comment). I agreed with the comment as a whole, but this portion really bothered me for a few days. 

If we spoke even one word, negatively or condemning about one person, without taking care of that by repenting and clearing that up, although God desires only blessing, favor and good on our behalf, He must rule on our guilt of sin.

 My Response:

SIN vs sins

Individual sins are a result of our inherited SIN nature. Jesus died to take care of SIN, not individual sins. Of course, individual sins are covered in that death, but it was the root He was after. Once you are born again, you will NEVER be ruled guilty of any individual sins, whether you remember to repent of them(it) or not. They are covered under the blood (past, present, and future). They are not irrelevant, but more on that in a moment. 

Repentance

Repentance has nothing to do with saying sorry or asking for forgiveness, repentance has to do with changing your WAY of thinking to align with His. Not just replacing your thoughts with His thoughts, rather, itβÄôs replacing your WAY of thinking, receiving information and processing information to become like His. 

Confession/Profession

Confession has nothing to do with claiming what you want or with confessing your sins, at least not directly. Confession/Profession means to βÄ€say the same thing asβÄù.  If GodβÄôs talking to you about your sin, and you say the same thing heβÄôs saying, then this would be confession. But if God is talking to you about your Righteousness in Christ Jesus and you are talking about your sin, then you are NOT confessing. You are not saying the same thing as Him.  Likewise, when it comes to the promises, unless God has said it, you can say it all day long and itβÄôs not confession, itβÄôs wishing. 

Vague Promises vs Specific Promises


Written = Vague

GodβÄôs Written Word provides many promises. They are, on balance, general/vauge about most things. It says he will never leave us or forsake us, that we will live long in the land if we honor our father and mother, that God delights in the prosperity of his servants, that he has a future planned full of hope and prosperity, that if we confess with our mouths and believe in our hearts we are savedβÄΠ.  On balance these are specific up to a point. Nothing in the written Word of God will promise you a specific outcome for a specific set of obedience. There are general principles, rock solid Truths, but not very many situation-specific/outcome-specific promises. 

Heard = Specific

For these, you must go beyond the Written Word, beyond rote learning, repetition, studyβÄΠ you must HEAR the voice of God speaking into your heart/situation. This gets harder. For this, you must hear. To hear, you must be listening. A myriad of things can hinder your listening/hearing.  You may be distracted by your own sins. Asking for forgiveness here could be a way to help clear your conscience and allow yourself to hear better. This does not mean that God needs you to repent in order to fulfill his promises or blessings, only that your dealing with this will help you clear your own conscience and help you hear. This mostly has to do with a need to renew your mind to His righteousness.


You may be dragged away by your own desires (James 1:14). You donβÄôt WANT to hear.¬†


You hear but donβÄôt like what you heard. You donβÄôt WANT to hear more.¬†


You may not have learned how to listen/hear him. Training/Practice¬†


You may have heard, but youβÄôre not sure how to know itβÄôs Him. Training/Coaching from others.¬†


Many other reasons you may not hear and/or obey what you hear, thus not receive from GodβÄΠ¬†


All of these have nothing to do with sins, directly. Sins can play a role in YOUR heart, not in GodβÄôs response.  

Darrell G. Wolfe








Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant |¬†Multipotentialite

title: Suicide: Can suicide send you to hell?
text:
The Blessed Life posted this video (here) βÄ€What Happens When You Commit Suicide?βÄù. This sparked a long on-going debate with strong feelings on either side. I love Ravi. HeβÄôs one of the best. IβÄôm kind of surprised he couldnβÄôt answer this one simply.  HereβÄôs the video, my answer below. 










 DISCLOSURE: Before reading my answer, please be aware of this. Suicide is a serious thing, with impacts not just for you but for your family, co-workers, and many others. If you are contemplating this, please seek help.¬†  There are many great resources, watch videos by Dr.¬†BobHamp,¬†seek a pastor our counselor, join a group. Suicide is a sign of depression and isolation. You need, above all else, connection with other believers. Not just to be physically around them, but an actual heart connection. Find someone, anyone, that you can talk to who will love on you with the Grace of God. Someone who can show you that thereβÄôs NOTHING in your heart that will prevent God and that person from loving on you.¬†  ThereβÄôs a second group, who donβÄôt care about the love, they just want to avoid the pain. The pain is real. Suicide is NOT¬†the answer. Let God heal that pain. Most of that pain is caused by a lie the Enemy is trying to sell you. Ask God: βÄ€What lie am I believing right now? What event caused me to believe that lie? And what Truth can you tell me about that?βÄù¬†  The answers I give are to address a very specific, targeted, theological question. They are not indented to justify your decision to take your life. Seek help, itβÄôs out there. If you donβÄôt know where to start, contact GatewayChurch in Southlake Texas. They can point you to some resources.¬†  Finally, if you are actually serious, like right now serious. Call 911 and tell them you are contemplating suicide and need to be taken in. They will help you prevent the action for now until you can get help.¬†  Check out my Resources page for Overcoming Depression.¬†  You can also contact: the¬†National Suicide Prevention Lifeline: Call 1-800-273-8255 




************

Now for the answer: 

The question¬†being asked is: Can suicide send you to hell?

 I donβÄôt believe this is a complicated question at all. ItβÄôs nothing to debate or get excited about. 

My Short answer: No.¬†Suicide will not send you to hell.¬†

 This very question is based on a simple, common, works-based theological error.  It can be worded in many different ways, here are some of the basics I saw in response. Each of the following commits the exact same theological error. Because they are in error, IβÄôll leave their name off from what they said, and simply address the error itself. 

All of the following are responses given using the same theological ERROR:




βÄΠ if you commit suicide you canβÄôt ask for forgiveness afterwardβÄΠthere is no second chance to repent. Suicide is a done deal and youβÄôre going to Hell !!




So if the Good Lord chooses to use you to Glorify himself in some manner, yet, you take your own life, in a selfish act of the flesh; you have glorified yourself and not the Lord. Tell to me, why Jesus would grant life to someone who does not want it.




You canβÄôt accept God and then not live by his word or have a relationship with him. Continuing to live in sin after having accepted God is not truly βÄ€acceptingβÄù Him. A relationship is a two-way street. You must follow GodβÄôs rules and HE will give you everlasting life. I truly hope this is a learning opportunity for you as per your beliefs you just stated you possibly have been sinning after accepting Jesus. This is no worries though just ask God for forgiveness and IβÄôm sure He will forgive you based on you not really understanding what He meant when He said βÄ€accept meβÄù




βÄΠsuicide is sin. All sin is based upon the flesh doing its will. I cannot make the choice to take my life which was given to me by God.




No dude. Suicide is cowardly. Suicide is not wearing the full armor of God. Suicide is lacking faith in God, in that He wants us to live a life of abundance. Jesus came to give life, not so you or I or anyone else can take it away at leisure. Remember, His ways are not our ways.¬†




Everyone wants to have their cake and eat it too. However, the cake was created to pour out on behalf of our brethren.




God gives life and takes. Murder is a sin. To take your own life is murder. Blessed are they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city. For without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie.




Saved or not saved, if you commit suicide you do not go to heaven. Read Ex. 20:13. Thou Shalt not commit murder. That is exactly what you are doing. Also if you can follow through with suicide than you are lost you arenβÄôt saved, those are all lies of the devil.







Correcting the error:¬†βÄ€WorksβÄù vs βÄ€GraceβÄù

 These are all reflections of an internal theological error, rooted into the church from day one. The early Jewish Christians made this error with their talk of βÄ€circumcisionβÄù. Then it found its way into the church through other avenues, and it still lives with us today.  ALL (100%) of the above comments have some element of a works-based mentality behind them.  They all say, in essence: 


IβÄôm saved through Jesus if I alsoβÄΠ.¬†


 The most common presentation is to hear the idea that βÄ€if I commit a sin and canβÄôt repent of it before I die, IβÄôm going to hellβÄù. Well, thatβÄôs religious nonsense and garbage. I can guarantee that youβÄôve committed sins at some point in your life that you donβÄôt remember and therefore cannot βÄ€repentβÄù of.

βÄ€Asking for forgivenessβÄù after each and every sin is irrelevant to salvation.¬†

 I know, that sounds like heresy, keep reading and youβÄôll get it. 

SIN vs sins

 There is a common misunderstanding between the condition called SIN and the individual acts called sins. We are in a state of being called SIN. We are born into this earth separated from God. Impure. Imperfect.  Jesus came, died, was buried and resurrected, raised and seated at the right hand of God the Father, and sent the Holy Spirit all for one reason. He is our replacement.  We either stand before God on the basis of our record or we stand before God on the basis of JesusβÄô record. He is the lamb, we cannot be. We will never be without blemish, and itβÄôs foolish for us to try to earn anything ever.  Some twisted version of works-based theology always tries to creep in and this point and tell us that Jesus died for our past sins, but we still have to keep repenting over-and-over-and-over to keep staying clean.  Hogwash.   Jesus died as our replacement. You will stand on His record or yours. Plain. Simple. Period.  

What about all the scriptures about repenting and asking for forgiveness?

 These have nothing so much to do with Salvation and more to do with our quality of life on this earth, and rewards beyond. No time to go deeper, thatβÄôs another post for another day.  βÄ€RepentanceβÄù has little to do with actions anyway.  Repentance isnβÄôt about changing your actions or thoughts.  Repentance has to do with changing your WAY of thinking, your thought process. And this is not a one-time event, or repeated event, or an event at all. This is a way of life. We are to constantly be renewing our mind to GodβÄôs way of thinking. And by getting his WAY of thinking, we eventually have new thoughts as a result, and then new actions. This is about how we live, and nothing to do with our salvation. 

So everyone goes to heaven without needing to accept Jesus?

No.¬†Again, you have a choice. You can stand on your record or His.  Whoever believes in him is not condemned, but whoever does not believe stands condemned already because they have not believed in the name of GodβÄôs one and only Son. John 3  ItβÄôs that simple. You either believe and accept that Jesus is your replacement or you donβÄôt.   

What now?

As I said in my disclosure, if you are struggling with this yourself, seek help.  If any of this is new information to you, youβÄôve been lied to by religion and religious doctrines of men. You need Freedom. There are many good Bible-teaching churches that will show you the Grace of Jesus.  IβÄôd also recommend you check out Bob Hamp, Christian Pastor and Counselor (MFT). He has some mind-blowing teachings on Freedom.  His website: BobHamp.com Facebook: Think Differently: Counseling, Consulting, Connecting Podcast: BobHamp¬†on CastBox, including a teaching on Depression.   Also, one person in the thread gave a link to John PiperβÄôs answer. ItβÄôs a decent answer and worth checking out.   Email Me@DarrellWolfe.com if youβÄôd like to chat more about this.  

Darrell G. Wolfe


 Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant |¬†Multipotentialite

title: Debt Disputes: What to do with collections, charge-offs, and unknown items on your credit report.
text:
Quite often, over the years, IβÄôve worked with people who had old items on their credit reports. Sometimes they donβÄôt even know what the items are for. Other times they disagree with the legitimacy of the report, or the reason the company says they still owe money.¬†





The following is my advice for any such individuals. Take it for what it is. IβÄôm not an βÄ€expertβÄù but IβÄôve been a loan officer for over 12 years and IβÄôve seen quite a bit.¬†



Step One: Debt Validation Letter


Step 2.1: Debt Validated/Settle


Step 2.2: Debt Not Validated/Still Not Removed.






Step One: Debt Validation Letter

See  TheBalance.com , I borrowed their letter and made it better. HereβÄôs my revised template:DateYour NameAddressCity, State ZipDebt CollectorβÄôs NameAddressCity, State ZipRe: [insert account number]This letter is sent in response to an item showing on my credit report. I am requesting that you provide validation of this debt. A validation of this debt includes a signed copy of the contract, statements, and notices. If you are unable to comply with a debt validation, you are required to remove this debt from my credit reports. Please provide me a letter confirming this was completed.If you do not comply with this request, I will may seek remediation through filing a complaint with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) and/or the Idaho Attorney GeneralβÄôs Office (ID AG). Civil and criminal claims may also be pursued.Sincerely,Your NameCC: ID AGCC: CFPB

********






Step 2.1: Debt Validated/Settle

If they validate the debt, and you are satisfied that you owe it. Work on settling the debt for less than you owe. 


Dave Ramsey Article on Debt Settlement


Letter Templates


Sample Lump Sum Debt Settlement Offer Letter::DateAcct Number: [insert account number]Credit Card Company NameCompanyβÄôs AddressCity, State and ZIP CodeRe: Requesting Payoff of my Credit AccountDear [insert company name]:This letter serves as my official offer to pay my past due balance of [insert type of product] with your company [list the name of the card company, account number Account Number]. As you are aware, I am unable to pay the full balance, however, I am able, at this point in time, to gather an amount that I believe will be an agreeable payoff amount.I would like to request that if I pay the amount of [insert total amount here], my account will be considered paid in full.If this is agreed upon, I would also request the following:My account be closed and no further interest or penalty charges would be applied.A written confirmation of your agreement to this proposal be sent to my address below.After agreeing to this proposal and receipt of your written confirmation, I will pay the amount of LUMP SUM AMOUNT via certified check within 10 days of receiving your letter.After you receive and post the payment, I request the following:An official letter indicating that my account is closed and my balance is $0.The account status as reported to the Credit Reporting Agencies be βÄ€CLOSED BY REQUEST OF CARDHOLDERβÄù or βÄ€PAID IN FULLβÄùThank you for your prompt attention to this matter. If you would like to discuss this further, please contact me by mail at the address below, by phone at the telephone number, or by email.I look forward to resolving this matter.Sincerely,Your SignatureYour Typed NamePhoneEmailAddressCity, State and ZIP Code

Step 2.2: Debt Not Validated/Still Not Removed.

If they do not validate the debt, or they do and you are not satisfied with the outcome, you may file a complete. The two best resources are the CFPB and ID AG.
