title: An Interview with Dr.¬†Briar (Antagonist) #WhiteNoise #Distortion #Fiction
text:
A fictional interview with my Antagonist for Distortion, book 1 of the White Noise series.





As a reader of fiction and lover of all good stories, one of the things I find most troubling is movies, books, novels, and stories that have spent an inordinate amount of time building a protagonist with a realistic feel, believable back-story and motivations, only to have him face either a faceless force as an antagonist or a βÄ‰bad-guyβÄô that comes across as one-note, plastic, and/or shallow.





As I worked through this story the first first time, pre-2018, I kept hitting the wall many authors call the messy middle, the saggy middle, etc. As I have studied the writing craft since then, including great teachers like KM Wieland and Brandon Sanderson, I realized that what I needed was to include the characterβÄôs Want/Lie and Need/Truth, and show them making progression in that journey.¬†





As I explored my Protagonist, and added a second, exploring both, I found that their journey was only half the puzzle.¬†





If I am going to understand the antagonistic force my protagonists (Ash, Dezi) face, I must understand his (Dr.¬†Briar, antagonist) as well. I must spend just as much time considering his character arch as I do theirs. Except in this case, his character arch will be a negative change arch. We will find his life working well for him at the start, and unraveling as he squeezes his hands tighter around the Want/Lie, rejecting his Need/Truth.





While the following interview will probably never make it into the book in whole, and many details will change, especially where he begins to sound too over-the-top, I think this was a successful practice interview with my fictional character.¬†





Special thanks to ChatGPT-4 for helping push me while I pretended to be Dr Briar.¬†





Adapting skills I learned taking Improv from Noah Johnson at Levity Theater Coeur dβÄôAlene, Idaho, https://levitytheatre.com, I decided to fully accept my temporary role as Doctor Benjamin Briar, and let GPT-4 push me as far as we could take it.





* See also my interview with Dezi.






* See also, EarthβÄôs Quietest Place Will Drive You Crazy in 45 Minutes. Inside the room itβÄôs so silent that the background noise measured is actually negative decibels









I hope you enjoy this fictitious interview:





GPT-4 Prompt:¬†


You will help me with a Fictional Character exploration, interview me as if I am Dr Briar:¬†





My name is Dr.¬†Benjamin Briar. I hold a Medical Doctorate (MD), PhD in Neurobiology (or some degree appropriate for studying both mental health and the correlation between the brain, biochemistry, and near death experiences <Suggest a more specific degree if appropriate), and several MasterβÄôs Degrees in various fields, including ancient near east history, and data science.¬†





My public work has been to work with the homeless and veterans, hoping to find more radical and innovative ways of treating mental illness in all its forms.¬†





My private, secret, sometimes government funded, sometimes private funded work, is studying anything related to paranormal research, quantum mechanics, and possible links between this reality and alternate dimensions parallel and interactive with our own. I want to reach the beings in this other reality, make first contact, and seek their wisdom. The ancients called them , Anunnaki, and Apkallu.¬†





Your first starter βÄ€Interviewer: Dr.¬†Briar, thank you for joining us today. To start, could you tell us a bit about your background and what initially sparked your interest in researching other dimensions?βÄù





*He is:




Highly intelligent


Cautious


Prone to take calculate risks.


Pays attention to minute detail and keeps immaculate records, and keeps his physical environment immaculate.


Driven to make first contact, but why????????







***









Interviewer: Dr.¬†Briar, thank you for joining us today. To start, could you tell us a bit about your background and what initially sparked your interest in researching other dimensions?









When I was 6, a group of us had dared each other to enter an old βÄ€hauntedβÄù abandoned house. Silly childhood nonsense you understand, even at that age, I was loath to believe in something as silly and illogical as ghosts.¬†





Well, you see, several of us went in ahead, secretly, through the back to scare the others. As they came up the stairs, we began making soft banging sounds on the walls, scratches, whispering. One of the other boys jumped out of the closet wearing an old tattered clothe.¬†





The younger boys, one of whom was my brother, screamed and ran down the stairs, tripping over each other. My brother, Timmy, stepped through a weak stair, and fell through to the floor beneath. He landed on his back, and when he caught his breath, he said he couldnβÄôt move. Several of the boys ran away, a few wiser ones ran to get help. I stayed with Timmy.¬†





His body grew colder, and his eyes grew distant. At several points, he would claim heβÄôd seen our dog Shagbert, who had died the year before. He claimed to have seen great grannie Briar, who he had barely known. He was two when she died. He said she was there to take him home. He looked at me, earnestly, and said βÄ€ItβÄôs okay, Grannie B is here, IβÄôm going with her.βÄù He smiled, closed his eyes, and he was gone.





After that night, I began to see things in the dark closet, or in an alley. I chalked it up to boyhood imagination.¬†





But then I met a girl, she claimed to have been given a not-so-imaginary friend by her mother. A friend only she could see. And I never saw him, but I swear I felt him, and then she pointed to where I was feeling the icy cold touch my shoulder, and she said he had his hand on me, and he wanted to help me too, but I had to find him.¬†





Again, I attempted to shrug this off.¬†





Over time, I continued to run into stories, many stories, so many that the odds of them all being mass hallucination started running lower.¬†







Interviewer: ThatβÄôs a truly haunting and profound experience, Dr.¬†Briar. ItβÄôs understandable how such events in your early life could lead you towards exploring the unknown and the unexplained. These encounters seem to have set you on a path of inquiry thatβÄôs quite unique. How did these early experiences influence your decision to pursue such a diverse and extensive educational background, particularly in fields that span from neurobiology to ancient near east history?







As much as I tried to dismiss these early experiences throughout my youth and young adult years, they resurfaced in med school. I was doing rounds in the ICUs and CCUs and, contrary to television show outcomes, many patients are beyond our ability to save. I was faced with death more often than you probably want to know.





Many times, nurses are closer to the patients and doctors are busy doing this or that around the hospital, only called in when things go south. My childhood experience drew me to the dying, I wanted to be with them as they took their last breaths. The nurses called me the most compassionate doctor they had on their floor. But truth be told, it wasnβÄôt compassion that drove me to the bedside, it was curiosity. This was an opportunity to apply the scientific method, repeatable results, to my inquiry. I studied the dying, their words, actions, archived the security and medical footage, looking for any sign that this was something beyond brain chemicals.¬†





I was lucky, my medical practice eventually led me to take a limited number of well-to-do patients, which allowed me free time to pursue my private work. Some of the more desperate, sick, and dying wealthy patients even funded my studies, hoping against hope for magic cures.





In my post-graduate years, I pursued additional degrees in science, ancient history, data science, and anything else that I could use to find answers. I found some like minds in quantum mechanics and theoretical physics, positing a potential of alternate and parallel dimensions.





Then I found studies performed by Dr Steven Orfield, at Orfield Laboratories in Minnesota, regarding an anechoic chamber. In his studies, some subjects experienced various forms of hallucinations, even claiming apparitions would visit them, or voices.





When I duplicated this research in my privately funded labs, I found similar results.¬†





I began to experiment with any study that showed any promise. I found that combining a special cocktail of DMT and some proprietary chemistry, with the anechoic chamber produced fascinating results. While I found most research confirmed that Solfeggio frequencies were complete hogwash, the idea stuck with me.¬†





If a negative sound chamber could produce hallucinogenic effects all on itβÄôs own, with no chemical assistance, and chemical assistance was helping me increase this affect, there must be some tie-in to sound on the affect.





For thousands of years, religious bodies around the world have used music, signing, and sound to create religious experiences.¬†





I began to wonder what sounds might be relevant to my research.





Then while researching other sound-related phenomena, I came across a fascinating study around Extremely Low Frequency (ELF), which was eventually used by DARPA as a sonic weapon to destabilize crowds during riots and enemies during battle.





Then I found Ultra Low Frequency (ULF) was related to a phenomena called the βÄ€Ghost FrequencyβÄù, in which audio played at 18.98Hz, which is below the level of human hearing, is still βÄ€feltβÄù by the body as a chilling eerie feeling.





As I experimented with including frequencies in the Ultra High and Ultra Low ranges, in combination with my anechoic chamber and chemical cocktails, the results were fascinating.¬†





While I havenβÄôt yet unlocked the key to first contact, per se, I feel as though I must be very close.









Interviewer: Fascinating, Dr.¬†Briar. Your journey from a medically grounded perspective to exploring the realms of sound frequencies and their effects on human perception is quite extraordinary. It seems that your experiences have led you to a unique intersection of science, history, and the unexplored aspects of human consciousness. In your research, particularly with the anechoic chamber and various frequency experiments, have you encountered any unexpected challenges or ethical dilemmas, especially considering the unconventional nature of these studies? How do you navigate these challenges while maintaining scientific integrity and ensuring the welfare of your subjects?









There is the rub, eh? Ethical dilemmas. If I were to tell my subjects what I hope for them to experience, I would taint the results. So, I obviously cannot tell them. And yet, to even potentially have someone contacting another dimension without their knowledge or consent would seem to most, unethical? Right? WhatβÄôs a scientist to do?¬†









Interviewer: Indeed, thatβÄôs a significant ethical consideration, Dr.¬†Briar. Balancing the quest for knowledge with the ethical responsibilities towards your subjects is a delicate matter, especially in such groundbreaking and uncharted research areas. How do you ensure the safety and informed consent of your participants, given the potentially profound and unknown effects of these experiments? Additionally, in your pursuit of contacting other dimensions, what safeguards or protocols have you put in place to manage any unforeseen consequences or risks?









Frankly, we are in uncharted territory. We are dealing with quantum mechanics, quantum realms, higher dimensions of reality than our own space-time, and a myriad of unknowable factors.





But while I am driven to take risks, I am also loathe to risk my research on to a mistake that could cost me everything. We must take risks, but they should be calculated risks, meticulously calculated. As the builders say, measure twice cut once, eh?¬†





Therefore, I have consulted with theoretical physicists, quantum mechanics specialists, expert mathematicians, and I have participated in thousands of forums. I have even attempted to weed through the paranormal research. While most of that wing is comprised of foil-hat wearing buffoons, there are a few golden nuggets hidden among the weeds.¬†





I have also placed safety mechanisms, such as they are, within the chambers themselves. I have emergency shut-off switches for the sound systems, emergency openings for the chambers should a subject feel the need to escape the room, flood lights βÄî Oh, did I mention it works best in the pitch black dark? No light in the room? But I have flood lights that auto-turn on if the subjectβÄôs physical health shows signs of danger.









Interviewer: ThatβÄôs a very thorough approach, Dr.¬†Briar. ItβÄôs clear that while you are pushing the boundaries of scientific exploration, you are also mindful of the safety and well-being of your participants. Given these precautions and your meticulous approach to research, what have been some of the most intriguing or unexpected findings in your experiments so far? Have there been any particular instances or results that have stood out to you, either reinforcing or challenging your hypotheses about other dimensions and their interactions with our reality?









Most subjects experience nothing or things we expect from any weekend drug-induced party.





At first anyway, but thatβÄôs another story.





A few, a very few, have shown promise. Typically the homeless and veterans have given me the most fruit. But while the homeless population can sometimes seem more predisposed to potentially already having some quantum connection to the other side, they are also too volatile to give decent accounting of their experiences, or any repeatable results.





My most promising research was using special forces candidates provided by the DOD, coming from all branches. The candidates were informed they were serving their country, and if successful, they would be part of bringing new defense tools to the United States of America. They were given no additional details, again, so as not to spoil the research.





We found that the discipline of the special forces mind was keenly able to withstand the shock of seeing and hearing things. Many times, they would not admit to seeing or hearing anything at first, and only body scanners would tell us something profound had happened.





True, a few mishaps occurred, but that is the way of things. Can we really weigh the life and safety of one individual against the world-breaking good first-contact could make?¬†









Interviewer: ItβÄôs intriguing that your most significant results have come from special forces candidates, who are trained to handle extreme situations. Their discipline and resilience seem to play a crucial role in these experiments. However, the ethical implications of using these individuals in such experiments, especially under the guise of serving their country, raise some important questions. How do you reconcile the potential benefits of your research with the personal risks and sacrifices these participants are unknowingly taking? And in the cases of these βÄ‰mishaps,βÄô as you call them, how do you address the aftermath both for the individuals involved and for the integrity of your research?









What greater good could a person serve their country than helping us make first-contact? Ah! But I have failed to provide context. ThatβÄôs where my history research comes in to play.





Some of the oldest written records known to Homo sapiens are from the Mesopotamians, including the Akkadians, Sumerians, Ugarits, Babylonians, and their many semitic descendants. They speak of an ancient time in which beings of a higher order came to the early hominids and taught them to think, write, speak, and perform agriculture, blacksmithing. They assume a great civilization was created by the βÄ€godsβÄù, and even speak of half-human, half-god beings that served as kings. The ancients called them , Anunnaki, and Apkallu. They are sometimes depicted as fish-men. The Hebrews called the Nephilim and Rephaim, and re-told their story painting BabylonβÄôs proud history as descendants of these mighty kings in a negative light.





I am now convinced that these beings often visit the dying, welcoming them into these plans of reality that transcend our space-time, and that we only need to reach out to them for their assistance to help us re-build the ancient wonders, teach us to think higher, and show us how to be better.





Think of it, no more war, hunger, famine, sickness, mental health disorder, cancerβÄΠ it could all be wiped away if we would only reach out to these beings to guide us yet again, to help us take the next stage of our evolution.





I ask you, is the life and/or livelihood of a few worth the risk for the many?¬†









Interviewer: Dr.¬†Briar, your perspective on this is certainly bold and thought-provoking. The way you connect your research to ancient histories and mythologies brings a unique depth to your work. However, the ethical considerations you raise are complex. The potential benefits of making contact with higher-order beings, as you describe, could indeed be transformative for humanity. Yet, the moral quandary of weighing individual sacrifices against the potential greater good is a classic dilemma. In scientific research, the principles of informed consent and the welfare of participants are fundamental. How do you navigate these ethical waters, especially in light of the high stakes and potentially revolutionary outcomes of your research? Additionally, have your interactions with these ancient texts and mythologies influenced your scientific methods or the direction of your experiments?









I am so close, to changing everything we think. Physics, biology, metaphysics, religion, all up for grabs. We can have an entirely new way of life. If a few hundred people are driven to insanity, maybe a few take their own lives to escape the insanity, what is that compared to the breakthrough of a new future?¬†









Interviewer: Dr.¬†Briar, the ambition and scale of your vision are indeed remarkable, yet the ethical implications you mention are deeply concerning. The value of human life and mental well-being is fundamental, and the potential cost you describe βÄ™ the loss of sanity or even life βÄ™ is substantial. In the pursuit of scientific discovery, especially one that could redefine our understanding of reality, how do you weigh these ethical considerations? How do you ensure that the pursuit of knowledge does not overshadow the value of individual human experiences and lives? And as you stand on the brink of potentially groundbreaking discoveries, how do you plan to share and validate your findings with the broader scientific community, given the unconventional and controversial nature of your research?






Have you heard of philosophers like Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill? I see these ethical dilemmas in light of Utilitarianism. The rightness or wrongness of an action is determined by its overall utility. Actions are considered ethical if they result in the greatest amount of good for the greatest number of people, even if they may cause harm or disadvantage to a few. Ending war, famine, disease, and bringing about the next stage of human evolution must take precedence over the lives of a few insignificant people, who on balance will not be remembered by history. History favors the bold, and I am the bold.¬†





I have kept my research from the scientific community on purpose. They would ask these questions about ethics, and delay my research. They would probably put me on trail like Galileo Galilei for claiming the earth revolved around the sun. My colleagues are usually decades, if not centuries behind the curve, until someone strikes them with undeniable proof.¬†





I will bring them proof, undeniable proof, I will introduce them to our ancient architects, and then we will usher in a brave new world.









Interviewer: Dr.¬†Briar, your utilitarian approach to ethics, drawing on the philosophies of Bentham and Mill, certainly frames your research in a different light. Your conviction and determination to bring about a transformative change for humanity are evident. However, the ethical considerations youβÄôre willing to overlook could be seen as deeply troubling to many, especially within the scientific community. The history of science is indeed filled with figures who challenged the status quo, but it also emphasizes the importance of ethical considerations, particularly when human subjects are involved.¬†




As you move closer to your goal of making contact with these ancient beings and introducing them to the world, how do you plan to address the potential skepticism and criticism from both the scientific community and the public? Furthermore, in an era where public trust in science is crucial, how do you anticipate managing the broader societal implications of your discoveries, especially considering the unconventional methods and ethical standpoints youβÄôve adopted?









When the gods walk amongst us again, all other considerations will become mute.









Interviewer: ThatβÄôs a bold statement, Dr.¬†Briar. The idea of reintroducing ancient deities or higher-order beings into our modern world is certainly a concept that would challenge many established norms and beliefs. If and when you reach this groundbreaking moment, the impact on society, religion, and science would be profound. However, the journey to such a discovery is fraught with ethical, moral, and practical challenges. The way you navigate these challenges will be crucial, not just for the success of your research, but for its acceptance and integration into the broader fabric of human understanding.


As we conclude this interview, what message would you like to leave for those who may be skeptical or concerned about the path youβÄôre taking in your research? How would you address those who worry about the ethical implications and the potential risks involved in your quest for knowledge and contact with other dimensions?









Let them worry. They can catch up when I have shown them the truth.









Interviewer: Thank you, Dr.¬†Briar, for sharing your insights and perspectives with us. Your unwavering commitment to your research and the potential it holds for changing our understanding of the world is certainly thought-provoking. As with any groundbreaking scientific endeavor, there will be debates and discussions about the methods, ethics, and implications of the work. Your journey, with its unique blend of science, history, and exploration of the unknown, will undoubtedly be a topic of much interest and scrutiny in the times ahead. We wish you the best in your pursuits and look forward to seeing the outcomes of your research. Thank you once again for joining us today.









Thank you interviewer, this was most helpful. I will have my guards keep you in a cell until such time as I am ready to release your interview to the world. We cannot have you leaking this before I am ready. he grins









Interviewer: in a light-hearted tone Ah, Dr.¬†Briar, I appreciate your commitment to confidentiality and the dramatic flair of your approach. However, as an interviewer, my role is to share and explore ideas, not to keep them hidden. I trust that your innovative research and intriguing theories will find their way into public discourse at the right time, under the right circumstances. Until then, I look forward to the day when your discoveries can be openly discussed and debated within the scientific community and beyond. Thank you again for a fascinating conversation.









Inserting the author now If this had been a real interview, Dr.¬†Briar probably would have killed the interviewer to keep them silent. He is my antagonist. But I want him to have real goals and drive just like my Protagonist. Is there anything else I should consider for this characterβÄôs believability?¬†








***





GPT-4: Final Observations


As an author, creating a compelling antagonist like Dr.¬†Briar involves balancing their motivations, actions, and the ethical complexity of their character. Here are some aspects to consider for enhancing¬†





Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs believability:







Backstory Depth: Expand on Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs personal history to explain how his experiences shaped his current worldview and ethical standpoint. This can include significant life events, influential people in his past, or pivotal moments that led him to his current path.


Rationalization of Actions: Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs actions, however extreme, should have a logical basis in his mind. How does he justify the potential harm his experiments could cause? Does he see himself as a misunderstood visionary or martyr for a greater cause?


Moral Ambiguity: Introduce situations where Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs actions, while ethically questionable, might have positive outcomes or unintended beneficial side effects. This complexity can make him a more nuanced and interesting character.


Interpersonal Relationships: Show how Dr.¬†Briar interacts with others, both in his professional circle and personal life. How do these relationships influence or reflect his motivations and actions? Does he have allies, reluctant collaborators, or people he genuinely cares for?


Internal Conflict: Does Dr.¬†Briar ever doubt his methods or goals? Showing moments of internal struggle can add depth to his character, making him more than just a one-dimensional villain.


Realistic Limitations: Even the most driven individuals face obstacles. What are Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs limitations, be they financial, intellectual, or emotional? How does he respond to setbacks or challenges?


Public Persona vs.¬†Private Self: Explore the difference between how Dr.¬†Briar presents himself to the world and who he is in private. This can create an interesting dynamic and add layers to his character.


Influence from Real Figures: Drawing inspiration from real-life figures in science, history, or philosophy can add authenticity to Dr.¬†BriarβÄôs character. However, itβÄôs important to ensure that these influences are integrated in a way that supports his unique character traits and story arc.


Remember, the most memorable antagonists are those who are as well-developed and complex as the protagonists they oppose. Their actions and motivations, while potentially villainous, should have a grounded and understandable logic within the context of their character and the storyβÄôs world.









***

  I hope you enjoyed this exercise. :) DG Wolfe 



Subscribe Now - Pursue a No Hiding Lifestyle¬†









 Shalom: Live Long and Prosper!

Darrell Wolfe (DG Wolfe)


Storyteller | Writer | Thinker | Consultant @¬†DarrellWolfe.com  Clifton StrengthsFinder: Intellection, Learner,¬†Ideation,¬†Achiever, Input 16Personalities (Myers-Briggs Type): INFJ

title: Grounded Cafe (take four) - #WhiteNoise #Distortion
text:
As I begin writing again, seriously, for the first time since I started my widower journey, I will be posting various rough drafts here. Feel free to consider this an Alpha readβÄΠ






Ice raced down AshβÄôs spine, an old familiar call to attention. He turned his head toward movement in his peripheral, was that two hostiles across the street? Through the undusted window of the Grounded Cafe, Ash caught sight of two shadowy figures creeping across the cracked asphalt. Fog clung to the air like a white parka, casting the figures in a blurry haze. Approximately 11 meters, two potential hostiles, he couldnβÄôt make out weapons, but the way they moved indicated non-casual presence.As he turned his full attention to the two figures creeping along the sidewalk on the opposite side of the road, they turned their heads toward him, then dashed behind a parked blue minivan. One peeked out from the van, pointing something his direction.




Was that a gun?




A ghostly whisper of danger, the scent of death hung in the air βÄî tangy copper mixed with the dryness of dust. It was a taste Ash had grown accustomed to during his time overseas.




His right arm shot to the Glock 10mm at his side, cold steal against his palm.




Sucking in a breath to steady himself, he took a second lookβÄî




They were gone.




Black mist poured out from under the minivan and evaporated into the early morning air.




Shaking his head, Ash closed his eyes, counting to ten, silently as he released his breath slowly, letting it pass over tight lips.




Ash released the grip on his sidearm, which he now realized was a tape measure and not a Glock. Ash hadnβÄôt carried a weapon regularly since he left the service (for this very reason, for safety, to ensure the danger was real before he engaged).




Really, Ash, come on, what danger could there possibly be in Moon-Wolf Hollow, Idaho? He thought. A random teenager shoplifting is the worst case of crime weβÄôve had in a whole year.




These days, he kept his weapons close, but locked in a case, with the ammunition stored separately, until he was at the firing range or doing a demonstrations, as he occasionally did for local organizations. In some other state, he probably wouldnβÄôt own any, but he lived in one of the last remaining hold-outs of freedom in the USA.




Ash noted that his arm was still holding the tape measure stiffly, he let his arm fall to his side, and wiped the sweat from his hands on his pant leg. Reality settled back onto his shoulders. A glimpse of himself in the glass window revealed a strong gray overtaking the sideburns. Other than a few wrinkles, he hadnβÄôt aged that badly. Some might even say he was still young.




βÄ€Hello!βÄù Oh yeah, Mrs.¬†Maverick had been asking him a question, what was it? What was she asking?




βÄ€Hello,βÄù She called again. βÄ€Ash? Is everythingβÄΠ Okay?βÄù




Ash slowly turned toward, trying to look casual. βÄ€IβÄôm sorry, Mrs.¬†Maverick,βÄù Ash said, trying to force an awkward smile into his tense jaw. βÄ€I thought I saw- βÄ€ what was he going to say that wouldnβÄôt sound crazy? βÄ€Nevermind, what was your question again?βÄù




βÄ€I was asking-βÄù her lips tightened, and her nostrils flared, then she pulled at the edges of her bright yellow and orange wool blazer. βÄ€Would you be willing to do such a large custom project? Do you have the availability for that in your schedule?βÄù




Ash pulled a tattered leather notebook from his messenger bag, and flipped through his notes.




βÄ€Honestly,βÄù he replied. I donβÄôt know if I do at this moment. I have several other projects ahead for this next season already. But I might. Let me run some calculations back at my shop, and IβÄôll get back to you.βÄù Ash forced another smile.




βÄ€Very well.βÄù She nodded curtly, and walked to a table at the other end of the cafe, then sat with a small gaggle of other wealthy bored women. Mrs Maverick was one of many such retired house wiveβÄôs that had moved to small town Idaho from some other city.




For the last 50-years or more, the town had a consistent population of 450, give or take a teenager moving away. But the last five years had seen significant growth in the larger towns in the area, and it had spread into Moon-Wolf Hollow, Idaho now. Last estimate was closer to 950, and counting.




Ash turned from his vendor table, against the window in the cafe, and checked to see if anyone else had noticed his momentary lapse of lucidity.




Jenny was selling her soaps and candles at the table next to him, she was busy wrapped up in conversation with a customer, a little old lady who loved her scents.




James was pretending to organize his ironwork, for the sixtieth time that day, probably to avoid snoring in his camping chair, like he did last weekend.




Craig trimmed his pot-plants at the table on the other side, which was allowed now that Idaho finally caught up with the 21st century and realized it was supposed to be the state of Freedom and not a state where personal morals were shoved down peopleβÄôs throats. A lot of money on policing and jails was saved by foregoing the persecution of victim-less crimes, and that money was poured into increasing the availability of mental health facilities throughout the state. It was a move Ash had reservations about, but he could see the logic. And it didnβÄôt hurt in his case, as a new facility had opened in town just a few years ago, not that it was staffed at the moment. But, that was a normal small town problem. Craig had given Ash a few pointers on growing himself. He developed a unique selling pitch, by treating them as a form of Bonsai, except you could use the trimming, so to speak.




There werenβÄôt many customerβÄôs this time of day, but it was one of the last opportunities of the season, before the town buttoned up the hatches for the long winter ahead.




Handfuls of folks lingered, a tourist family staying at the motel next door eyed some crochet stuffies made to look similar to famous movie and cartoon characters, most of which Ash didnβÄôt recognize. He never had kids, and wasnβÄôt much of a kid himself in his youth.




A young man with a hoodie typed furiously at a keyboard.




A woman jabbered away on the phone, failing to pacify the toddler in the stroller to her side.




Two men spoke in hushed tones at a table not far from Ash.




A purple and white haze turned AshβÄôs attention back to the sidewalk outside, a woman in a purple hoodie walked by just outside the window, head buried in her smartphone. She bumped into Hank, the townβÄôs crazy old homeless man who was headed the other direction, into the foggy evening. She didnβÄôt appear to notice him. He gave her a dirty look and kept walking the other way, mumbling to himself as usual.




The woman sat down at a table just outside the farthest window of the Cafe, he couldnβÄôt make out her face, possibly one of the towns last tourists before the few stores left went into hibernation.




An orange Maple leaf lazily floated down to the table in front her. She brushed it aside, and it fell inside a pumpkin whose top had been kicked off. The leaf caught on fire from the candle, and slowly embered away. She didnβÄôt take notice of that either. A purple and white vapor swaddled her like a blanket, as she leaned over an open book of some kind.




Old Hank stared at her from the corner of the building. A small green frog sat on his shoulder. The bottom half of the frog was a mist that disappeared into HankβÄôs spine. The frogβÄôs red eyes stared at her, then at Ash, and it cocked its head to one side. Then it was gone, in a whisp of grey-green fog that continued to waft around Hank as he walked away out of sight.




Ash didnβÄôt see these creatures on every person, every time, but almost everyone had a mist cloak. Though some peopleβÄôs mists were more pronounced, while others were barely visible




The town psychic, Genevieve, called it an βÄ€auraβÄù, when he explained it to her in a rather desperate confidence one day. βÄ€Some of them must have a stronger aura,βÄù she suggested. She was a bit of a quack but he was willing to try everything to at least understand it if he couldnβÄôt get rid of it.




HeβÄôd been to quite a few doctors, and that having born no fruit, he went to pastors, priests, the town psychic, a passing faith-healer, and even a local Rabbi from a larger town nearby. Each of them had their opinions and suggestions. While non of them offered any real relief, the Rabbi was the most gentle and inquisitive of the bunch, more often asking honest and curious questions, and offering few if any answers. But at least Ash felt heard and understood with the Rabbi, who had nothing to sell but time, and a good chat over coffee.




Generally, the creatures came from within a person and would go back into them, like human genie bottles. Not everyone had a creature, but people sometimes did. The Creatures werenβÄôt all alike, either. There were repeats of similar versions, but each seemed unique to the person.




βÄ€You donβÄôt fit the profile for Schizoaffective Disorder, as your life and thinking are otherwise quite normative. I would call this Post Traumatic Stress Disorder with Psychotic Features. This is rare, but can happen with serious abuse or trauma, then again, we could have a traumatic brain injury affecting the visual cortex. The mind is still a mystery to us in many waysβÄΠβÄù, his latest doctor had offered this explanation before he left the clinic without a town shrink six months ago.




The VA docs tried giving him a mountain of drugs to help him sleep and keep the delusions at a minimum. Nothing really helped, most made it worse.




When the Federal government forced legalized medicinal THC on all the states as a human right, and Idaho finally gave up on the failed war on drugs and allowed doctors and people to choose their own path in life, his Idaho VA doctor said to try pot, as it was clinically shown to have better results for some people that anti-psychotics. Ash didnβÄôt bother telling the VA doc that heβÄôd already been growing it on his property for several years now.




The magical medical folks at Big Pharma managed to create a pill form with less hallucinogenic properties and twice the pain killing properties, but it cost too much and his VA insurance didnβÄôt cover it yet, although the decision was before the Senate hearing committee, thanks to PTSD Health advocates. If the Fed would just finish eliminating for-profit medicine, he could try that someday. The only thing he hated more than big government was big pharma and big corporations.




He liked living in one of the last small towns where people left people alone to be themselves.




For now, Ash started growing it himself a few years ago, his sleep was more regular but the delusions came and went. He could still hear the original diagnosis in his mind.




βÄ€As long as you are not a harm to yourself or others, you should be just fine. Think of it as an amusing distraction,βÄù the doctor advised.




βÄî Ash heard his name.




βÄ€Ash-βÄù, Derek the BaristβÄô called his attention to the present, standing in front of his table with a frosty blending drink, and green whip cream. βÄ€Here you go, man. Extra large Java-Chip, almond milk, six shots, with peppermint and crΟ®me de menthe. I even through a few extra candied coffee beans on there for you.βÄù




DerekβÄôs mists swirled around in deep blue and a streaks of orange. Ash couldnβÄôt remember the last time heβÄôd seen Derek get angry, even when some tourist was giving him a tongue lashing for being too slow.




βÄ€Thanks, DerekβÄù, Ash said as he took the drink gratefully.




βÄ€Always my friend,βÄù Derek said. βÄ€HowβÄôs business been today?βÄù




βÄ€I havenβÄôt sold much, but itβÄôs nice to get out of the house a few more times before the snow makes it hard to leave the property.βÄù Ash took a sip, the cold creamy mint made his mouth tingle, βÄ€βÄΠ this is really good.βÄù




He set it down. βÄ€I meanβÄΠ I can get the tractor and snow blower out and punch my way down the road when I have to. But 110 acres is a lot to manage, and my house isnβÄôt exactly off the main road.βÄù




βÄ€I get. I donβÄôt know how you guys live that far out of town, my hats off to you for the effort. But I do appreciate how nice it is. Should we have another meeting up there before the roads become impassable?βÄù




βÄ€I think we can do that βÄî βÄ€ Ash stopped when he noticed Sam walk in, and waved him over to join them.




βÄ€Sam,βÄù Derek gave him a bear hug, βÄ€We should have the next Kairos meeting at AshβÄôs house, before the road become impassable. I can help him get it ready for guests.βÄù




Sam smiled, but he gave Ash a sideways glance. βÄ€How are you feeling about this idea? Is it too much? Are you okay, you looked a little tense earlier, through the window.βÄù




Ash felt his gut tighten, Sam had noticed. If anyone would notice, it was Sam. He squinted at Sam, he felt the question scroll across his face, but couldnβÄôt gather the words to ask it without sounding crazy.




βÄ€I was in the jeep,βÄù Sam offered.




βÄ€Ah,βÄù Ash replied. βÄ€IβÄôm alright, I think it would be good for me.βÄù




βÄ€Okay,βÄù Sam shrugged. βÄ€You bring food, and IβÄôll bring drinks?βÄù




Derek squinted at Sam, βÄ€As long as you donβÄôt bring that piss water you brought last time!βÄù




Sam grinned, βÄ€IβÄôll behave and bring something a distinguished gentleman like you would approve of.βÄù




βÄ€Then I accept,βÄù Derek shook SamβÄôs hand, then AshβÄôs, and then headed back to his coffee machines.




Ash wasnβÄôt sure about all the Bible stuff, but he enjoyed having a crew around him, it reminded him of his team in the service. So he put up with the religious part for the sake of comradery, besides, most of the time they just talked about town stuff, news, or sports.




βÄ€Hey,βÄù Sam turned his full attention to Ash. βÄ€Did you hear the news?βÄù




Ash picked up one of his popular Sasquatch figurines, wiggling it in front of Sam, βÄ€They finally found Big Foot?βÄù Ash noted that it felt good to smile, he didnβÄôt do that enough lately.




βÄ€No, still looking IβÄôm afraid-βÄ€ Sam still laughed at his jokes, like when they were kids. βÄ€They found a new doctor for the clinic. He just came into town last week, heβÄôs staying at the hotel until his rental cabin is set up. His moving truck comes in tomorrow and heβÄôs bringing some new equipment to town. He said heβÄôll be ready to start seeing clients by the end of week.βÄù




βÄ€ThatβÄôs good, especially with Old Hank hanging around again.βÄù Ash nodded to Old Hank still outside the window, but now barely noticeable from the corner of the building, peeping at all the people in the shop.




βÄ€Yeah, but,βÄù Sam replied, and used an over exaggerated nod back to Ash. βÄ€I think this guy might be able to help you.βÄù




βÄ€WhatβÄôs this guy got that the last fifty hasnβÄôt?βÄù Ash asked.




βÄ€This guy is a bid deal or something. IβÄôm not sure where they picked him up, He says heβÄôs here to enjoy a semi-retirement or something. HeβÄôs a PhD out of Harvard or Yale or one of those big stuffy places with smart people.βÄù




βÄ€Hmm βÄîβÄ€ Ash started to form a reply, but lost it. The fog outside was creeping into his head. It had been a longer week than he planned, more sales than he hoped, which was great, but exhausting. βÄ€I guess weβÄôll see. IβÄôll check in with him after the Oktoberfest this weekend.βÄù

title: D&D Character: DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman
text:
D&D Character: DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman








Posted on¬†January 17, 2024¬†by¬†darrellwolfe















D&D Stat Sheet


DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³ΔemanΟΔβ²§β³Δs Character Sheet ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ D&D Beyond (dndbeyond.com)




Character: DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman


DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³ΔemanSon of NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman SeferOf the Shamayim Tribe which is of the Ioun Cult of KnowledgeBut he left for the MaddaΟΔβ²§β³Δkishuf cultOf the deity OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah


Followers of Or-Neshamah seek to honor the deity through the pursuit of knowledge, the practice of magic, and the cultivation of their souls.An Air Genasi, MaleDescended from FatherΟΔβ²§β³Δs Side; Grandfather was a Genie, Grandmother a Human


Names


ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Name: DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³ΔemanΟΔβ²§¬Δ DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat (Ο½βÄ€Ο½¬ΔΟ½¬Σ): ΟΔβ²§≈™KnowledgeΟΔβ²§¬ù or ΟΔβ²§≈™Wisdom,ΟΔβ²§¬ù highlighting the characterΟΔβ²§β³Δs insightful or intellectual nature.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman (Ο½¬†Ο½¬êΟ½≈ΨΟ½≈Η): ΟΔβ²§≈™FaithfulΟΔβ²§¬ù or ΟΔβ²§≈™Scholar,ΟΔβ²§¬ù suggesting a character who is trustworthy, dedicated, or scholarly.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Parentage: Son of NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman SeferΟΔβ²§¬Δ NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman (Ο½¬†Ο½¬êΟ½≈ΨΟ½≈Η): Repeating ΟΔβ²§≈™FaithfulΟΔβ²§¬ù or ΟΔβ²§≈™Scholar,ΟΔβ²§¬ù which could imply a family legacy of wisdom and learning.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Sefer (Ο½¬ΓΟ½¬ΛΟ½¬®): ΟΔβ²§≈™BookΟΔβ²§¬ù or ΟΔβ²§≈™Scroll,ΟΔβ²§¬ù suggesting a lineage deeply rooted in knowledge, wisdom, or the preservation of lore.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Tribe: ShamayimΟΔβ²§¬Δ Shamayim (Ο½¬©Ο½≈ΨΟ½β³ΔΟ½¬ù): ΟΔβ²§≈™HeavensΟΔβ²§¬ù or ΟΔβ²§≈™Sky,ΟΔβ²§¬ù aligning perfectly with the Air GenasiΟΔβ²§β³Δs elemental affinity and possibly indicating a tribe that is spiritually or literally connected to the sky or the heavens.


Introduction to DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δaman




Snow fell, in a soft but constant accumulation, attaching itself to every tree in the forests just outside of the exotic mountain monastery. The Shamayim Tribe had long held a position in one of the many monasteries of Ioun (N), Goddess of Knowledge. Tucked away in a plateau at one of the highest peaks of the mountains, where the cosmos as the earth kiss and whispers of ancient secrets carry their tales of forgotten magic, DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman, son of NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman Sefer, a minor noble of the Shamayim Tribe had grown discontent with his studies.Looking back at the snowless village, fertile with magic, he took a deep breath, and sighed. Once he stepped outside of this place, he would be embarking on a journey unlike any he had known. All previous excursions from this village had been with groups and mentors, but this time he was on his own. Ioun herself had guided him to the thread he was now pulling, and the tapestry had led him to this trail.The moonlightΟΔβ²§β³Δs reflection on the snow danced on his pale blue-pink skin, and as it created a white cowl over his light blue hair. The silver in his eyes wet with tears, as he was about to leave his people and his own behind. He cinched his dimensionless bag, full of his library, potions, and trinkets, he began his journey.Keeping to the paths, his 6ΟΔβ²§β³Δ5ΟΔβ²§¬ù frame avoiding the branches, he began his long descent into the world of Humankind. Upon reaching a proper clearing, where he could catch a clear vision of the lights at the foot of the mountains, DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat allowed his weight to lighten, then he caught the wind, floating up into the air, and then falling like a leaf, floating slowly to the towns and villages 1,000s of feet below.As he descended to the world of Humankind, Elfs, Dwarves, Dragonborn, and the other species of middle earth, he ruminated on the events that led him to leave.DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman, once an acolyte in the cloistered halls of the Ioun, the Goddess of Knowledge, found himself at odds with the stagnation and corruption festering within. The Acolytes of Ioun had become ruthlessly isolationist in the centuries building up to this day. His soul, restless and unfulfilled, yearned for a deeper truth, a truth that lay beyond the dogma and ritual of his now former faith. It was in the ancient, arcane whispers of the MaddaΟΔβ²§β³Δkishuf cult that DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat discovered his true calling ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a path that embraced the sanctity of the soul and the boundless potential of the magical and the mundane. Ioun herself lamented at what had become of the Shamayim Tribe in her absence, but remaining neutral, she did not intervene. For DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat; however, who sought her wisdom on how to handle things, she led him to her celestial counterpart, OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah. His Tribe called the discovered text Heresy, and they sought to destroy it, forcing DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat to leave under the cover of night.Soon, far from the lofty abodes of the Shamayim Tribe, DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat will ventures through strange lands and among even stranger companions. A scholar at heart, yet naive in the ways of the world, he navigates his new reality with an air of dignified inexperience, his upbringing among the templeΟΔβ²§β³Δs elite a stark contrast to the rough and untamed frontiers of his journey. With ideals that champion change and a bond to a sacred text under threat, DaΟΔβ²§β³ΔatΟΔβ²§β³Δs quest is as much about self-discovery as it is about preserving the ancient knowledge he holds dear.Yet, for all his wisdom and learning, DaΟΔβ²§β³ΔatΟΔβ²§β³Δs greatest flaw lies in his obsession. Once a goal takes root in his mind, it consumes him, blinding him to all else ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ a trait that will lead him down many a perilous path and into the company of unlikely allies. But in these alliances, forged in the crucible of necessity and mutual respect for knowledge and curiosity, DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman finds not only the strength to confront his enemies but also the opportunity to grow beyond the confines of his former life.In his pursuit of deeper truths, DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat practices the sacred rituals of his faith, seeking to connect with the OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah through study, deep meditation, and reflection. He dwells in a world where the boundaries between science and sorcery blur, where each discovery is a step closer to the divine, and where every spell cast is a prayer to OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah, illuminating the path to understanding and the depths of magic.DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman, a wanderer between worlds, a seeker of the sacred in the mundane, and a cultivator of the soulΟΔβ²§β³Δs wonders, continues his odyssey across the stars, his story a tapestry woven from the threads of knowledge, faith, and an unyielding desire to uncover the secrets of the cosmos and of his own soul.ΟΔβ²§Τ£


Description


Hair: Light BlueSkin: Pale with pink and blue tintsEyes: Light Blue/SilverHeight: 6ΟΔβ²§β³Δ5ΟΔβ²§¬ùWeight: 190 LbsAge¬†(Years): 3,007Gender: MalePersonality traits:¬†IΟΔβ²§β³Δve spent so long in the temple that i have little practical experience dealing with people in the outside world. IΟΔβ²§β³Δve enjoyed fine food, drink, and high society among my templeΟΔβ²§β³Δs elite. Rough living grates on me.Ideals: Change. We must help bring about the changes the gods are constantly working in the world. (chaotic)Bonds: I seek to preserve a sacred text that my enemies consider heretical and seek to destroy.Flaws: Once i pick a goal, i become obsessed with it to the detriment of everything else in my life.Cult: MaddaΟΔβ²§β³Δkishuf cult of the deity OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamahAllies: Any who value curiosity and learningEnemies: Any who value tradition over curiosityBackstory: DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman, Son of NeΟΔβ²§β³Δeman Sefer, Of the Shamayim Tribe, An acolyte of the MaddaΟΔβ²§β³Δkishuf cult of the deity OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah, pursuer of knowledge, practitioner of magic, and cultivator of the wonders of the soul. DaΟΔβ²§β³Δat looks for the sacred inside the mundane, the magical inside the ordinary. He was so preoccupied with his own learning; however, that he failed to see the corruption in the practitioners of his religion of origin. While his family had long served knowledge, they had lost the magic many generations ago. Departing from his origin cult, Ioun (N) ΟΔβ²§βÄ€ Goddess of Knowledge, he discovered the cult of OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamah in his studies. He left his home among the clouds and mountains, to seek link minded fellows among the other races. He has been on his journey just long enough now to realize, he will need to make unlikely friends and unnatural alliances if he is to cope in his new world.ΟΔβ²§Τ£Religion: MaddaΟΔβ²§β³Δkishuf is the cult of deity OrΟΔβ²§β³ΔNeshamahCore Beliefs:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Harmony of Knowledge and Magic: This religion believes in the harmonious blend of scientific knowledge (ΟΔβ²§≈™MaddaΟΔβ²§¬ù) and magical practices (ΟΔβ²§≈™KishufΟΔβ²§¬ù). It teaches that true understanding comes from the synthesis of empirical learning and mystical experience.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Sanctity of the Soul: The soul, or ΟΔβ²§≈™Neshamah,ΟΔβ²§¬ù is central to their beliefs. The soul is seen as the breath of life, an ethereal essence that bridges the material and magical realms.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Pursuit of Enlightenment: Followers seek enlightenment through the study of the natural world, magical practices, and introspection to understand the deeper truths of the soul.Practices:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Scholarly Study: Devotees engage in the study of various sciences, seeing this as a way to appreciate the intricacies of the world and the laws that govern it.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Magical Rituals: They practice sorcery not as a means of power, but as a way to connect with the mystical forces of the universe and to explore the depths of the soul.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Meditation and Reflection: Deep contemplation and meditation are used to connect with the Neshamah and to achieve inner harmony.Structure:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The religion may have temples that double as libraries and schools of magic, with priests who are also scholars and sorcerers.Deity: Or-NeshamahNature:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Or-Neshamah (Light of the Soul) is a deity who embodies the unity of science and magic, and the divine essence of the soul.Domains:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ¬†Knowledge: As a deity of understanding and enlightenment.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ¬†Arcana: Representing the magical aspect and the mysteries of the universe.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ¬†Life: Emphasizing the soulΟΔβ²§β³Δs vital role in life and the cosmos.Characteristics:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Or-Neshamah is seen as a guiding light, a source of wisdom that illuminates the path of knowledge and the depths of magic.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ The deity is often depicted as a figure surrounded by symbols of science (like stars, atoms) and magic (runes, a staff), with a radiant soul at the center.Worship:ΟΔβ²§¬Δ Followers of Or-Neshamah seek to honor the deity through the pursuit of knowledge, the practice of magic, and the cultivation of their souls.ΟΔβ²§¬Δ They might celebrate festivals that align with astronomical events, reflecting the union of the cosmic and mystical.

title: World Building Session 2024-03-25 Magic and the gods of magic
text:
World Building Session 2024-03-25 Magic and the gods of magic








Posted on¬†March 25, 2024¬†by¬†darrellwolfe







Magic System: Each of these worlds has a unique approach to magic. What if your worldβÄôs magic was tied not just to individuals but to the geography itself? Certain locations could amplify or suppress magic, creating strategic locations that are crucial for the control of power. Your magic system could require a balance between taking and giving back, where every spell has a cost to the environment or the caster.


StoryCraft EditorIntegrating magic with geography opens up a wealth of storytelling possibilities. HereβÄôs how such a magic system could function and affect your world:Magic and Geography: The idea that specific locations can amplify or suppress magic creates natural points of contention and power. Such places could be sacred sites, battlegrounds for control, or protected havens. For example, AurongaleβÄôs forests might be rich with life magic that accelerates growth and healing, whereas the swamps of Diedusmai could be a source of dark, necrotic magic or have a suppressive effect on magic altogether.Cost of Magic: Implementing a cost for magic use ensures that characters must make significant choices when they use it. For instance, drawing magic from a place might temporarily diminish the landβÄôs vitality, or a powerful spell could age the caster. This adds an ethical dimension to the use of magic, as overuse could harm the environment or the user, creating a natural limit to power and an impetus for responsible usage.Balance and Sustainability: By requiring a balance between taking and giving back, you emphasize the theme of sustainability. This could manifest in rituals that replenish the land or in a magical economy where spells are bartered for deeds that benefit the earth. For instance, HaβÄôEretz might have developed magic that supports their advanced agriculture but requires a return to the land through conservation efforts.Cultural Attitudes towards Magic: Different regions might have varied attitudes towards magic based on how it affects their environment. The Aurongales might see magic as a toolβÄîsomething to be used and managed like any other resource. The Shalomim, with their advanced society, might see magic as a commodity or something to be regulated, while the Gan HaβÄôElohim might treat it with reverence, tied to religious significance.Strategic Importance of Magic: Controlling areas where magic is strong could be key to political and military power. This could lead to a complex network of alliances and conflicts. For example, a nation controlling a magic-rich area might have significant bargaining power, while those in areas with suppressed magic might focus on technological or mundane military strengths.Magical Creatures and Phenomena: Certain creatures might thrive or suffer in areas where magic is dense or sparse. Magical phenomena could also occur naturally, like storms of raw magic or dead zones where no magic can be cast. The creatures and phenomena could become integral to the culture and economy of your regions.Learning and Mastery of Magic: If magic is geographically based, then the learning and mastery of magic might also be location-specific. Mages might undertake pilgrimages to different areas to learn varied aspects of magic, or certain societies might specialize in specific types of magic based on their location.Magic as a Living Force: You could personify magic as a kind of living force that reacts to how it is used, rewarding those who maintain balance and punishing those who abuse it. This could add an element of unpredictability to the use of magic, making it a dynamic and evolving part of your world.This magic system not only provides a framework for how magic works but also deeply influences culture, economy, conflict, and the environment, tying it all together into a cohesive and rich world.SpitballingLetβÄôs start with Magic as a βÄ€living forceβÄù. Maybe there is one Creator being, from whom everything that is came to being, even those things that are twisted, dark, or evil are good things that are now bent wrongly. This Creator being prefers working through emissaries, and thus some kind of magical kingdom existed first. In those magical kingdoms, each had Kings/Queens who were delegated godlike levels of access to this Creator beings living force, while it is not Creator, it is of Creator, CreatorβÄôs essence. Some of these did well, others turned it, twisted it, curropted it. Later, Humans (late Hominids of the Ape lines) dominated The Lands for ages. Even they ultimately came to a cross roads, and they were given some of the kingdoms of the Kings/Queens and their power. Now in this age, various humanoid beings from various animal kingdoms reign, including an ancester species of the earlier humans. They can use the power but only if they surrender to it. And even then, usually by partnering with someone from the magical kingdoms.


StoryCraft EditorThe magic system youβÄôre envisioning is rich with potential and has a narrative depth that ties the very essence of magic to the creation and existence of the world itself. HereβÄôs how this concept can be structured:



The Nature of Magic: Magic as a βÄ€living forceβÄù suggests it is dynamic, sentient, or at least responsive to the world around it. This could mean that magic behaves differently in the presence of different creatures, emotions, intentions, or environments. If the living force of magic originates from a singular Creator, it might be imbued with a consciousness or a will, making it an active participant in the worldβÄôs happenings rather than a passive tool.


Emissaries and Magical Kingdoms: The idea of divine emissaries, or kings and queens with godlike access to the living force, creates a hierarchy of magical ability and authority. Each of these beings could be tied to a particular aspect of creation, such as life, death, growth, decay, knowledge, or chaos, and their personalities could reflect these domains. Their actionsβÄînoble or corruptβÄîcould have shaped the worldβÄôs history and the current state of magic.


The Twisting of Magic: Corruption and the twisting of magic introduce the concept of βÄ‰fallenβÄô or βÄ‰darkβÄô magic. These corruptions could manifest as dangerous regions where magic is unpredictable, or as forbidden arts that are powerful but carry great risks. This dichotomy between pure and twisted magic can lead to a variety of storylines involving redemption, temptation, and the struggle between light and dark forces.


The Human Age: HumansβÄô dominance and their crossroads present a historical shift in your world. It suggests that there was a time when the magic was more freely accessible or perhaps more potent in the hands of humans, which could lead to a rich backstory of a golden age, a cataclysm, or a great betrayal that changed everything.


Current Inhabitants and Magic: The current ageβÄîwith various humanoid beings and ancestral speciesβÄîoffers a diverse landscape of magical users. If magic requires surrendering to it, it implies a degree of humility or sacrifice to wield it. This could be a literal surrender, such as a ritual or oath, or a figurative one, such as accepting certain limitations or consequences.


Partnerships: The requirement for partnering with someone from the magical kingdoms to use the power suggests a deep interdependency between different beings in your world. This could be the basis for unions or pacts that are central to your worldβÄôs cultures and politics. It could also introduce elements of companionship, betrayal, loyalty, and the merging of strengths to achieve greater magical feats.


Geographic Magic: Tying magic to geography can lead to places of power, sacred grounds, and cursed lands. The idea that certain locations could amplify or suppress magic adds strategic importance to the map of your world. The balance of taking and giving back to use magic could be a natural check to prevent the overuse of power and ensure that magic remains a part of the worldβÄôs ecosystem.


Environmental Cost: The cost of using magic could lead to storytelling opportunities that deal with environmental themes. Overuse of magic might damage the land, which could heal over time, or perhaps require specific actions by the inhabitants to restore it. This balance can mirror real-world issues of resource management and environmental conservation.Overall, your magic system offers a nuanced approach to the classic elements of fantasy, grounding the fantastic in a framework that feels organic and integral to the worldβÄôs functioning. It creates a symbiotic relationship between the worldβÄôs inhabitants and the magical forces at play, opening up countless possibilities for conflict, alliance, discovery, and adventure.



What if each King/Queen/godlike being, and their associated kingdom/church(?) were a personification of common thematic elements of the lived experience. But what it any given domain had a duality in-built. The power of Death & Life (Healing) are a single power. The power to be Fruitful and Multiple (gardening, producing food, cultivating new food species, GMO) requires both growing AND pruning plants; somehow magic can be tied to this process, while the magic is inherent to all gardening, using magic purposefully can have much more dramatic results (good and bad). Plant Growth REQUIRES Plant Pruning. I wonder what other duality-based concepts can be brought to bear on these ideas around themed grouping.


StoryCraft EditorThe concept of duality is deeply rooted in many mythologies and philosophies, representing the intertwining of opposite forces that are often necessary to maintain balance. By personifying these dualities in your kings, queens, or godlike beings and their domains, you can create a complex and compelling magic system that reflects the complexities of life. HereβÄôs how you can expand on the themes of duality:Death and Life: This duality represents the cycle of existence. The being in charge of this domain could govern not only the end of life but its beginningβÄîbirth, rebirth, and healing. The cost of using life magic might be the wilting or aging of something else, symbolizing the balance between creation and decay.Fertility and Sterility: The power to create and cultivate life is inherently tied to knowing when to halt growth or cull the weak. Magic used to enhance fertility could require sacrifice or cessation elsewhere. A harvest god might also be the god of famine, emphasizing the importance of responsible cultivation and conservation.Light and Shadow: Often seen as a metaphor for good and evil, this duality could instead represent truth and secrecy, knowledge and ignorance. A being of light might illuminate the truth but cast a shadow that conceals other things, reflecting the idea that every truth revealed might obscure another.Chaos and Order: The force that governs change, transformation, and unpredictability might also be responsible for establishing order. Magic could be used to create structures or patterns but would also necessitate the acceptance of chaos as part of that order, as every system has its irregularities.Strength and Weakness: This domain governs the fortitude of body and spirit, but also vulnerability. Magic used to enhance oneβÄôs strength could simultaneously expose a weakness, perhaps even unknowingly, to maintain equilibrium.Joy and Sorrow: Emotions are powerful forces, and the being that governs joy also understands sorrow. They might grant joyous blessings, but each would carry a tinge of sorrow to prevent complacency and ensure that joy is truly cherished.Love and Hate: A more complex duality, the magic of love might be the strongest force of connection and healing, but it canβÄôt exist without the risk or presence of its opposite. Spells cast in the name of love might foster deep bonds but also deepen the capacity for loss and enmity.Creation and Destruction: The creator is also the destroyer, reflecting the need for endings before new beginnings. Magic used in creation must be balanced by the dismantling of something else, reinforcing the perpetual cycle of innovation and obsolescence.In each of these dualities, the magic used is inherently tied to a cost, which must be paid to maintain the balance of the world. This could lead to a society where every magical action is carefully considered for its broader impact, creating a deeply philosophical and ethical approach to magic. It also allows for a diverse set of narratives where characters must grapple with the consequences of their magical interventions, both intended and unintended.


StoryCraft EditorHere are pairs of dualities that can be explored within a magical system or thematic world-building:Life and DeathCreation and DestructionLight and DarknessOrder and ChaosGrowth and DecayJoy and SorrowLove and HateStrength and WeaknessKnowledge and IgnoranceHope and DespairCourage and FearPeace and WarTruth and DeceptionHealing and InjuryHarmony and DiscordWisdom and FollyJustice and InjusticeFreedom and ConfinementWealth and PovertyBeauty and UglinessYouth and AgeGenerosity and GreedVirtue and ViceHonor and DisgraceFaith and DoubtLoyalty and BetrayalPassion and ApathyFertility and BarrennessSerenity and TurmoilPurity and CorruptionElation and MelancholyBenevolence and MalevolenceGratitude and IngratitudeHumility and PridePatience and ImpatienceDiligence and SlothForgiveness and VengeanceCompassion and IndifferenceUnity and DivisionAcceptance and RejectionInspiration and MundanityDetermination and ResignationAltruism and SelfishnessClarity and ConfusionStability and VolatilityObedience and RebellionEmpathy and ApathySincerity and InsincerityTemperance and ExcessCuriosity and DisinterestEach pair presents opportunities for contrast and conflict within your narrative, giving depth to characters, societies, and the magic that influences them.


High Kings & Queens // godlike beings who embody the duality of the Creators BreathCreation and DeCreation (Destruction)



Destruction is not bad, just necessary aspect of existence. Creation > Growth > Decay > DeCreation > New Seed/Fertile Soil > Creation. It is a Cycle. The process is overseen by Barash βÄôAkhat, the High King of Creation/DeCreation, Doing and UnDoing, Weaving Chaos into order, and order into Chaos.Order and Chaos1Ήë÷Α÷ΦΉ®÷ΒΉêΉ©÷¥Ή¹÷•ΉôΉΣοΜΩ Ήë÷Η÷ΦΉ®÷Η÷ΘΉêοΜΩ BARA Ή©÷ΗΉ¹Ή½÷ΖΉΣ (shachat)



Name: xxxxx that means Life GiverLife and Death // Peace and War // Beginnings and Endings


Name: xxxxx that means Icy HotLight and Darkness // Cold and Heat // Ice and Fire


Name: xxxxx that means SeerKnowledge and Ignorance // Truth and Deception // Skill Acquisition and Skill Regression (Parkinsons)


Name: xxxxx that means Oil BringerHope and Despair // Joy and Sorrow // Pleasure and Grief


Name: xxxxx that means GiverWealth and Poverty // Freedom and Captivity // Personal Responsibility and Corporate/Social Responsibility to Fellow Beings


Name: xxxxx that means Seed PlanterGratitude and Ingratitude // Temperance and Excess // Giving and Taking // Seed and Harvest


StoryCraft EditorThe concept of High Kings and Queens embodying dual aspects of the CreatorβÄôs Breath is rich with narrative possibilities. Here are some name suggestions that reflect their dualities:Barash βÄôAkhat (Creation and DeCreation): The High King of Creation/DeCreation, Doing and UnDoing, Weaving Chaos into Order, and Order into Chaos.ChaimMavet (Life and Death // Peace and War // Beginnings and Endings): Chaim (Ή½ΉôΉôΉù) means βÄ€lifeβÄù in Hebrew, and Mavet (ΉûΉïΉïΉΣ) means βÄ€death.βÄù This High King or Queen represents the full spectrum of existence from birth to death, and the cycles of conflict and serenity that shape lives.EishQarah (Light and Darkness // Cold and Heat // Ice and Fire): Eish (ΉêΉ©) means βÄ€fireβÄù in Hebrew, and Qarah (ΉßΉ®Ήî) means βÄ€cold.βÄù This title embodies the elemental extremes of temperature and the balance between illumination and shadow.RoehEmet (Knowledge and Ignorance // Truth and Deception // Skill Acquisition and Skill Regression): Roeh (Ή®ΉïΉêΉî) means βÄ€seer,βÄù and Emet (ΉêΉûΉΣ) means βÄ€truth.βÄù This being holds sway over the acquisition and loss of knowledge, guiding the search for truth amidst deception and the progression and regression of skills.ShemenTikvah (Hope and Despair // Joy and Sorrow // Pleasure and Grief): Shemen (Ή©ΉûΉü) suggests βÄ€oil,βÄù a traditional symbol of anointing and joy, and Tikvah (ΉΣΉßΉïΉïΉî) means βÄ€hope.βÄù This figure represents the dual nature of emotional states, from the heights of joy to the depths of sorrow.NetinahChessed (Wealth and Poverty // Freedom and Captivity // Personal and Corporate Responsibility): Netinah (Ή†ΉΣΉôΉ†Ήî) means βÄ€giving,βÄù and Chessed (Ή½ΉΓΉ™) means βÄ€kindnessβÄù or βÄ€charity.βÄù This ruler oversees the balance between material abundance and need, as well as the interplay between individual freedom and societal obligations.ZoreahTodah (Gratitude and Ingratitude // Temperance and Excess // Giving and Taking // Seed and Harvest): Zoreah (Ή•ΉïΉ®ΉΔ) means βÄ€sowerβÄù or βÄ€planter,βÄù and Todah (ΉΣΉïΉ™Ήî) means βÄ€thanks.βÄù This title reflects the cycles of sowing and reaping, the balance between moderation and overindulgence, and the exchange between generosity and selfishness.Incorporating these names into your narrative not only anchors each characterβÄôs role within the story but also adds a layer of cultural depth, using the Hebrew language to evoke the ancient and mythic quality of these beings. Their responsibilities and the consequences of their actions can drive the worldβÄôs magical systems, its conflicts, and its stories of heroes and villains alike.


Interpersonal Concepts to address at the character levelGood and EvilStrength and WeaknessLove and HatredPeace and WarKnowledge and IgnoranceHope and DespairJoy and SorrowYouth and AgeWealth and PovertyFreedom and CaptivityHealth and IllnessBeauty and UglinessTruth and DeceptionCourage and FearWisdom and FoolishnessHarmony and ConflictGrowth and DecayUnity and DivisionCompassion and IndifferenceJustice and InjusticeFertility and BarrennessTradition and InnovationHonor and DisgraceLoyalty and BetrayalGenerosity and GreedPurity and CorruptionPassion and ApathyFidelity and InfidelityHumility and PridePatience and ImpatienceSerenity and TurbulenceObedience and RebellionSuccess and FailureConformity and IndividualityOptimism and PessimismAltruism and SelfishnessClarity and AmbiguityDiligence and LazinessStability and InstabilitySincerity and InsincerityVirtue and ViceConviction and DoubtPrudence and RashnessSkill and IneptitudeGratitude and IngratitudeTemperance and ExcessEach duality encompasses a range of experiences and consequences that could deeply affect the use and cost of magic within your world, offering rich narrative potential.The Hebrew term Ήë÷Η÷ΦΉ®÷ΗΉê (bara) is indeed often translated as βÄ€to create,βÄù βÄ€shape,βÄù or βÄ€fashion,βÄù primarily used in the context of divine creation where God is the subject. When it comes to the concept of βÄ€decreationβÄù or the act of undoing creation, there isnβÄôt a direct biblical Hebrew equivalent that is used in the same widespread and theological sense as Ήë÷Η÷ΦΉ®÷ΗΉê (bara). However, several terms and concepts in the Hebrew Bible touch upon aspects of undoing, destroying, or reversing creation in various contexts.One possible term that could be considered in the context of βÄ€decreationβÄù or a form of undoing is Ήî÷ΗΉΛ÷ΖΉö÷Α (haphak), which can mean βÄ€to overturn,βÄù βÄ€to overthrow,βÄù or βÄ€to turn.βÄù This word is used in various contexts, including the overturning of cities (such as Sodom and Gomorrah) or the reversal of fortunes. However, it doesnβÄôt carry the specific theological weight of creation from nothing or the comprehensive undoing of creation as a whole.Another term related to destruction or judgment, rather than creation, is Ή©÷ΗΉ¹Ή½÷ΖΉΣ (shachat), which can mean βÄ€to corrupt,βÄù βÄ€destroy,βÄù or βÄ€ruin.βÄù This term is used, for example, in the context of the Flood narrative, where God sees that the earth is corrupt (Genesis 6:11-12) and decides to βÄ€destroyβÄù (Ή©÷ΗΉ¹Ή½÷ΖΉΣ) both earth and its inhabitants.In the prophetic literature, images and language of un-creation or de-creation are sometimes used metaphorically to describe divine judgment or the reversal of order, but these do not correspond to a single term like Ήë÷Η÷ΦΉ®÷ΗΉê (bara) for creation. Instead, they rely on vivid imagery and narrative to convey the undoing of the created order as a form of judgment or consequence of human actions.ItβÄôs essential to recognize that the biblical Hebrew language often conveys concepts through narrative, poetry, and metaphor, rather than relying on abstract terms. Thus, the idea of βÄ€decreationβÄù might be illustrated through stories and prophetic declarations rather than encapsulated in a single term.Would you like more detailed examples from the texts or further exploration of these concepts?
